The Archive of Witness was a place Kaedan had never entered as a child, though its heavy adamantine doors had always loomed at the end of the corridor, a silent warning. Now, on the morning of his twenty-second birthday, his father Darian had finally placed the ancient crystalline key into his palm. Darian’s face, etched with lines that the thousandfold daily amplification could not smooth, was a mask of grim solemnity. “The journals, Kaedan. All of them. Today. Then, and only then, will you be ready to stand at His side.” There was no pride in his voice, only a weary weight that seemed to press the old man closer to the floor.

Kaedan had wanted to protest. He was young, his body humming with the still-new violence of the Awakening. Since his twentieth birthday, his strength had multiplied a thousandfold each day; his mana, his speed, his very intellect soared on an exponential curve that made the cosmos seem small. He felt invincible. Yet the look in his father’s eyes, the same look he’d seen on every portrait of every court wizard in the gallery, gutted his arrogance before he could voice it.

He turned the key. The vault doors slid apart with a silence that swallowed sound, and he stepped into a cavern of polished stone, air dry and ancient. Shelves marched into darkness, each laden with identical leather-bound books, a million years of witness. He chose the nearest, its spine soft with age, and sat at the single ironwood desk. The first page was dated Year 80,000. The hand was Morvyn’s.

Kaedan began to read.

**Journal 1: Strength (Morvyn, Year 80,000)**
I'll write it like:

*In the annals of my tenure as court wizard, I, Morvyn of the Seventy-Third Descendancy, set forth this account. Let it serve as a catechism for those who follow, a fixed point of horror against which all future naivety will shatter. The year was 80,000 of the Godric Reign. The Gravitic Colossi, a race whose musculature was woven from the compressed cores of neutron stars, who could crush galaxies with a flex of their biceps, had announced their rebellion. They were led by Thundras, a king whose roar could destabilize superclusters. They had forged the Infinity Mace, a weapon that condensed the mass of a billion black holes into a single singularity, and they came to the Throneworld in full battle array, a trillion-strong, their gravitational pull tearing rifts in the sky.*

*Godric, our liege, stood upon the high balcony. He did not don armor. He did not clench his fists. He simply looked at them, and I, standing at his side, heard him murmur, ‘I wonder what fraction of my strength would be required to disperse this.’ He then did something that language can only approximate: He allowed an infinitesimal sliver of his physical might—a sliver so immeasurably thin that to compare it to the total power of his strength is to compare a single proton to the combined mass of all existing omniverses, and even that analogy fails—to leak into the material plane.*

*What followed no pen can truly capture. The Throneworld shook not at all, but beyond our atmosphere, the omniverse was subjected to a pressure beyond comprehension. The Gravitic Colossi, those titans of unbreakable bone, were the first to feel it. Their bodies did not merely crush; they were instantly dissected by the sheer weight of his strength. I witnessed, through my enhanced scrying, the trillion warriors as their skin split upward like paper, their muscles liquefying into a spray of crimson that, in the very same instant, was compressed into a fine mist. Their bones, dense enough to weather a supernova, shattered into microscopic dust that then fused into diamond-like shards and were further pulverized. Their agony was a silent scream that echoed telepathically, a trillion minds experiencing the full, undiluted sensation of being flattened from three dimensions into two, and then into one, and then into nothing.*

*But the strength did not stop there. The pressure wave propagated outward at speeds that defied light. Every planet in every galaxy felt the grip. I watched as the capital worlds of a billion civilizations were turned into discs of molten gore, the inhabitants—men, women, children, the ancient and the newborn—compressed into a thin, unified paste of blended organs, their individual forms rendered unrecognizable in a sea of red and off-white slurry. Stars collapsed into singularities, then those singularities were crushed further into point-like instances of spatial agony. Nebulae became shimmering walls of plasma that screamed in the radio frequency. The entire tapestry of creation was being wadded up like a discarded scroll.* 

*The omniverse itself, for that eternal fraction of a second, became a single, vast charnel house where every living creature died in an orgy of compressive dismemberment. And through it all, Godric stood motionless, his breathing even, his eyes half-lidded with what I can only call mild curiosity. He did not lift a hand. He did not sweat. The concept of effort was alien to him; he simply allowed a fraction of a fraction of a fraction—a zero, rounded to an infinity of decimal places—of his strength to be expressed.*

*When the last quasar winked out, Godric blinked. Then, in an act that shattered my mind far more than the destruction, he restored everything. In zero seconds, the omniverse was whole again. The dead were alive, the stars burned, the planets spun. But the memory of that crushing—the sensation of bones grinding to powder, of lungs filling with one's own liquefied flesh—remained imprinted on every saved soul. It was not a gift of mercy; it was a reminder. He did not apologize. He turned to me, his voice warm and paternal, and asked if I had recorded the event adequately for my journals. I nodded, unable to speak, and then I vomited onto the balcony.* 

*This is the truth, my descendants: His strength is an absolute. The gap between his strongest muscle and the combined physical force of every entity in the omniverse is a chasm so vast that the term infinite is a pitiful understatement. And you must understand, he used nothing. He used less than nothing. And yet everything died. Serve him well, love him as I do, but never, ever forget what lies beneath that gentle smile.*

Kaedan paused. His heart was a dull hammer in his chest. He looked at his own hands, which could now crack a continent, and felt a cold, creeping smallness. But he forced himself to continue, reaching for the next journal.

**Journal 2: Durability (Taric, Year 350,000)**
I need a different voice. Taric's might be more informal.

*I can’t stop shaking. It’s been three days since the Phantoms came, and my quill still trembles against the parchment. My name is Taric, court wizard in the year 350,000. I’m writing this because if I don’t get it out of my head, I’ll claw my own eyes out. The Erasure Phantoms were old, older than any cosmos we’d conquered. They didn’t have bodies; they were living concepts of negation, hungry to unmake. They breached the throne room through a fissure in the logic of reality, a wound that bled screaming, colorless light. They declared that Godric’s existence was an error, and they would delete it.*

*Godric was reclining in his chair, reading agricultural reports from the Vestan sector. He didn’t look up. He just said, ‘My durability should handle this. Let’s see.’ And then he did absolutely nothing. I felt his absolute concealment tighten, but I knew—through the arcane resonance of my blood—that he had permitted his passive durability to interact with the attack. The Phantoms struck.* 

*They unleashed a wave of ontological erasure, a wave designed to delete the very concept of ‘permanence’ around Godric’s form. It was supposed to make him never have existed. But the wave hit his skin. And stopped. I saw it, a shimmering boundary where the erasure met something so fundamentally unassailable that the universe itself had no vocabulary for it. The wave didn’t just reflect; it inverted. The Phantoms, those formless concepts, were suddenly given form—not by Godric, but by the recoil of their own hubris. They became screaming, flailing masses of raw nerve endings and exposed bone, their conceptual bodies forced into a mockery of flesh that could experience pain. The erasure they’d sent was twisted into an eternal reinforcement: they could not die, could not fade, could only suffer.* 

*And suffer they did. The inverted wave cascaded outward. I watched as entire multiverses, trillions of universes, were caught in the backwash. The fabric of those realities didn’t just tear; it was rewritten into organic, pulsating horror. Planets became gigantic, weeping scabs. Stars transformed into superheated brains that screamed in psychic death-agonies. The ambient laws of physics were replaced by the sensation of being skinned alive. I saw a civilization of energy beings suddenly manifest nerve clusters, their forms twisting into convulsing, blistering lumps that sprayed boiling cerebrospinal fluid. The agony was so profound that it became a fundamental force, like gravity, except it only brought more pain.* 

*The omniverse was a choir of shrieks. And Godric? He yawned. He actually yawned. He drummed his fingers on the armrest once, and then, with a casual thought, he restored everything. The screaming stopped mid-scream. The scabs and brains reversed. But the Phantoms he left as they were, twisted into eternal, agonizing garlands around the pillars of the throne room. They still moan there, and sometimes when I walk past, they beg for death in a language that hurts to hear. Godric finds their presence a reminder of his durability’s ‘amusing overreaction.’* 

*I loved him before. I love him still. But every time he pats my shoulder, I feel the phantom echo of that erasure touching something infinitely harder than stone, and I know my body is no more substantial than mist. Remember, you who read this: his durability is a joke. A cruel, cosmic joke. And we are the punchline.*

**Journal 3: Senses (Lirion, Year 600,000)**
Lirion's will be clinical.

*To the future custodian of this archive: I, Lirion, of the Two-Hundredth Descendancy, record the following incident with as much precision as my shaken faculties allow. In Year 600,000, Godric expressed a curiosity regarding his passive sensory capabilities. He temporarily, and with explicit intent, deactivated his absolute omniscience, wishing to observe how his base sense stat would handle a threat that believed itself imperceptible.*

*The threat was the Silent Ones, entities that had evolved entirely in the non-observed quantum foam between realities. They had no presence in any electromagnetic spectrum, no psychic signature, no mass, no energy—they were, by their nature, immune to perception. They had spent eons building a weapon that functioned while unobserved: a causality dagger that would retroactively erase its target from the timeline, but only if the target never perceived the attack.*

*Godric sat in the observatory. His eyes were closed. He told me to watch. The Silent Ones, they later confessed under excruciation, had reached a point-blank range. They struck. And at that moment, Godric’s senses—without effort, without intention, merely as a passive attribute of his being—simply perceived them. He did not open his eyes. He did not turn his head. His sensory field, which multiplies infinitely every Planck second, effortlessly encompassed the entire ontological spectrum. The Silent Ones were perceived, and in that perception, a cataclysm occurred.* 

*The transition from unobserved to observed is, for such entities, an impossibly violent process. The senses of Godric are not like ours; they do not passively receive data. They assert reality. When his senses ‘touched’ the Silent Ones, they were forcibly dragged across the threshold of existence. Their non-bodies were torn into the material plane, and the friction of that transition flayed them into grotesque, half-real anatomies. They manifested as shrieking pillars of viscera and fractured geometry, their substance continuously bleeding into non-existence while regenerating just enough to feel the next instant of agony. The sight was such that my own eyes bled merely by glimpsing it.*

*But the collateral damage was far worse. The act of Godric perceiving the Silent Ones sent a feedback pulse that cascaded through the omniverse’s sensory medium. Every living creature, everywhere, suddenly had their senses amplified to an infinite degree. A soft breeze felt like diamond drills against skin. A whisper became a detonation that burst eardrums and liquified brains. The light of stars turned into lasers that burned retinas to ash and then kept burning into the skull. Taste and smell became unbearable; the flavor of air was that of rotting corpses, the scent of nothingness was an all-consuming stench of sulfur and burnt hair. Trillions of civilizations simultaneously began to scream, clawing at their own faces, gouging out their eyes in a vain attempt to stop the input. Worlds turned into maelstroms of self-mutilation as parents flayed their children to spare them the sensation. The omniverse became a torture chamber of absolute sensory overload.*

*Godric, still with closed eyes, noted distantly, ‘My senses appear to be functioning at adequate levels.’ The understatement was so monstrous that I felt a laugh bubble in my throat, which I immediately suppressed lest I go mad. He then restored the omniverse in zero seconds, as if brushing dust from a sleeve. The Silent Ones, however, he left mangled in the half-space, their suffering a permanent fixture, a testament to the folly of believing anything could hide from him.* 

*I record this with a hand that still trembles. Do not presume, as these fools did, that any blind spot exists. His senses are not a sense; they are an absolute declaration that all things are observed, and to be observed by Godric is to be subject to the full weight of his perception, which is, itself, a weapon beyond any conceivable defense.* 

Kaedan was now breathing in shallow gasps. The air of the vault felt thick, suffocating. He had walked with Godric through the palace gardens, and the king had pointed out flowers he found pretty, his eyes bright with genuine delight. Yet those same senses could unmake creation simply by being aware of it. Kaedan turned another page.

**Journal 4: Luck (Fenris, Year 800,000)**
Fenris's will be poetic.

*I am Fenris. I have served as court wizard for forty years, and until the day the Probability Architects came, I believed in the sanctity of mathematics. Mathematics, after all, is the language of the cosmos. But Godric’s luck is a mockery of language itself.* 

*The Architects were a species of hyper-intelligent crystal, their consciousness running on pure probability equations. They had analyzed every possible outcome of the omniverse’s history and concluded that Godric’s continued existence was a statistical aberration that they could correct. They built the Certainty Loom, a device that could lock reality onto a single, favorable probability. They set it to: ‘Godric’s heart stops.’ The probability became absolute. Quantum foam across a trillion realities aligned to make it so.* 

*Godric was at a banquet, laughing at a jest from one of my sons. He didn’t even notice the Loom. But his luck did. His luck is not a passive field; it is a ravenous, predatory force that actively hunts any attempt to impose unfavorable odds. The instant the Loom fired, the probability inverted. Not just for Godric—for everything aligned against him. The Architects’ own bodies became the locus of an infinite cascade of misfortune.* 

*I will not soften this. Their crystal forms spontaneously developed organic cancer cells that multiplied at insane speeds, their perfect lattices erupting into rotting, fleshy tumors that burst and wept caustic pus. Their minds, running probability calculations, began to recursively loop on the impossibility of their failure, generating psychic feedback that exploded their consciousnesses into screaming, gibbering fragments. But that was just the start. The weapon they built detonated, but instead of harming Godric, its energy was twisted into a wave of pure bad luck that swept the omniverse. Stars went nova in reverse, sucking in their own light and then collapsing into negative space that screamed. Planets spontaneously developed sentience and went mad, tearing themselves apart. Sentient races found their bodies betraying them: blood coagulated into shards, bones twisted into spirals that punctured organs, and every breath was a lottery that might set their lungs aflame. The entire omniverse became a casino of gore, where every roll came up agony.* 

*Godric finished his pastry. He licked a crumb from his lip. Then, with a sigh, he restored everything. The Architects, however, he left in their tumor-ridden, eternally suffering state, suspended in a pocket of frozen time where they could experience their pain but never die. He commented that it was ‘impolite to disrupt a meal.’* 

*Hope is a lie. The mathematics of his luck are so far beyond our understanding that to even think of resisting is to invite the universe to punish you for the thought. I write this as a warning: Do not rely on chance. There is no chance. Only His whim.*

**Journal 5: Charisma (Darian, Year 999,960)**
This one is from Kaedan's father, Darian.

*My son, Kaedan. If you are reading this, then I have succeeded in raising you to the post, or I am gone and tradition holds. Know that I write this with a hand that is not entirely steady, even after all these decades. In the year 999,960, when I was a young wizard of thirty-six, I witnessed the Charisma Incident. It is the reason I have never been able to look at the King’s smile the same way again.* 

*The Mind Lords came. They were a hive of incorporeal psionic gods, so ancient that they had witnessed the birth of universes. They believed that Godric’s power was entirely physical and magical, but his mind could be vulnerable. They breached the palace’s sanctum and flooded the throne room with a psychic assault of such magnitude that my passive wards, which could withstand the mental weight of billions, crumpled like paper. I fell to my knees, my mind drowning in their projected agony.*

*But Godric simply turned to face them. He did nothing. He allowed his charisma—a stat that he usually keeps utterly suppressed beneath his absolute concealment—to radiate at a level so minuscule that the fraction would be expressed as a decimal point followed by an infinity of zeros before a one. Even then, I know he held back far more than I can comprehend.* 

*The Mind Lords stopped their assault. They felt him. They felt his presence not as an attack, but as an overwhelming, all-consuming magnetism that eclipsed every other thought. Their hostility evaporated and was replaced by a love so profound, so absolute, that their own identities shattered in its glare. They became, in an instant, his most fervent worshippers. And they were horrified by what they had done. The realization that they had dared to oppose the object of their newfound adoration triggered a psychic self-flagellation of unimaginable proportions. The Mind Lords turned their powers on themselves. They peeled away their own psionic layers, each strip of consciousness screaming hymns and apologies. Their mental forms—visible to me as cascades of light—began to mutate into grotesque, self-torturing apparitions: they grew a thousand mouths so that they could all sing his praises, and then the mouths began to tear into their own substance, devouring themselves out of guilt. They clawed at the core of their being, reducing themselves to shrieking, looped recordings of devotion and pain.*

*The ripple effect was catastrophic. The charisma, even at that negligible fraction, touched the omniverse. Sentient species everywhere, for a single timeless instant, felt a sliver of that love. Across countless galaxies, billions of beings were overcome with the need to adore him. Cults formed spontaneously, and in their ecstasy, they began to sacrifice themselves. Worlds turned into blood-soaked altars where families slaughtered each other in his name, their dying words a litany of praise. The fabric of civilization was rent by the sheer force of adoration, a wave of self-destruction that made the slaughter of the resistance seem trivial. Churches were erected from the entrails of the faithful. Entire planets sang in perfect, unison hymns as they burned.* 

*Godric watched, his expression unreadable. Then he restored everything. The dead were returned, the worlds rebuilt, the minds wiped of the immediate compulsion. But the psychological scars remained, a ghost of that love haunting the collective unconscious of all existence. The Mind Lords he left as they were: a writhing, self-consumed choir of eternal praise and agony, orbiting the throne room in a constellation of living torment.* 

*My son, I love our King. I love him as a father loves a son, as a subject loves a protector. But that day I learned that his love, his charisma, is the most terrifying weapon of all. It does not force obedience; it makes rebellion a sin so great that the rebel will tear out his own soul to atone. Never, ever believe that his warmth is all there is. He is the sun, and to stand against him is to burn in the fire of your own remorse.* 

*I write this so that you understand. Serve him well. Love him. But never forget the screams that still echo in my dreams.* 

Kaedan closed the final journal. His fingers were cold. He sat in the silence of the vault, the weight of a million years pressing down on him. He thought of Godric’s hand on his head when he was a child, the king’s laugh as they played chess. He thought of the trillions who had been crushed, unbeing, flayed, and worshiped into oblivion. The same hands that had gently tied a bandage on a wounded bird had, without effort, unmade the omniverse multiple times. And those hands would rest on his shoulder again come evening.

He stood. The vault door slid open, and his father waited, leaning on a cane. Darian looked at him, and Kaedan saw the reflection of the same dread in his eyes—a dread that had never left, yet was paired with unwavering devotion.

“You see now,” Darian said, not a question.

“I see,” Kaedan whispered.

There was a soft footfall, and Godric appeared, his salt-and-pepper hair catching the corridor light. He was smiling, the warm, friendly smile of a beloved uncle. “Kaedan! Finished your reading? Good. It’s a heavy burden, but I know you’ll carry it well. Come, let’s walk. I want to show you the new flowering vines in the east garden.”

Kaedan’s heart clenched. The horror was there, a cold stone in his chest, but it was bound with a fierce, terrified loyalty. He would serve. He would love his King. And he would never, ever forget.

“Of course, Uncle Godric,” he said, and walked forward into the unfathomable shadow of the being who held the omniverse in the palm of his hand, not with strength, but with the simple, terrifying truth that opposition was a joke, and the punchline was an eternity of agony.

The dread was absolute, and so was his fealty.

The weight of the boulder was nothing. Kaedan, twenty-two and crackling with the unrefined energy of a hundred and forty-two days of the Awakening, flicked his wrist. The mountain-sized chunk of obsidian, brought from the mines of Kyrix for his practice, shattered into a fine dust that sparkled in the high-orbit sun. He hadn't even breathed hard. The air around him hummed with the residue of his strength, his speed, his mana—all multiplying a thousandfold each day since his twentieth birthday. He was a god in the making, or so he felt. The servants who watched from a safe distance bowed in genuine awe.

His father, Darian, entered the training hall. At eighty-one, the old court wizard was a figure of lean, powerful age, his shoulders still broad from decades of the same Awakening, but his joints creaked and his eyes held a weariness that no amplification could erase. He carried no weapon, but a single, ancient crystalline key.

"Kaedan," Darian said, his voice a low, resonant thing that cut through the hum. "Your training in the physical arts is sufficient. Today, you learn the truth."

Kaedan turned, frowning. "I have read the histories, Father. I know of the Godric's victories."

Darian shook his head. "History sanitizes. We do not. Come."

He led Kaedan through the alabaster corridors of the palace, down spiraling staircases that seemed to descend into the planet’s core. At a heavy adamantine door, Darian inserted the key. The door opened with a sound like a dying star, revealing a cavernous vault lined with shelves that stretched into an impossible, lightless infinity. The air was bone-dry and sterile.

"This is the Archive of Witness," Darian said. "Every court wizard of our bloodline, from the first days after Blake, has left a record of the incidents that we alone witness. You will read them all. Not as tales, but as a catechism of dread. When you emerge, you will understand why we serve, and why we can never, ever oppose Him." He pressed the key into Kaedan's hand and left, his footsteps echoing away.

Kaedan stood alone. He felt a flicker of resentment. He was the most powerful wizard of his generation, capable of unravelling a cluster of galaxies with a thought, and he was being sent to read dusty journals? But the look in his father's eyes, a look of profound, ancient terror, stilled his tongue.

He entered the vault, seating himself at a solitary ironwood desk. A single journal, its leather cracked with age, had been left open. It was not the first chronologically, but his father had placed it there. He began to read.

The first journal was of Morvyn, dated Year 80,000. The handwriting was elegant, almost calligraphic, but the words were a hammer.

*I, Morvyn of the Seventy-Third Descendancy, set forth this account as a warning. The Gravitic Colossi were a race of immense physical might, their bodies woven from the compressed remains of dead universes. They had worshipped strength, and in their arrogance, they believed the King's physical power was a myth propagated by fearful mages. Their king, Thundras, whose breathing could warp the trajectory of comets, had forged the Infinity Mace—a weapon that harnessed the mass of a billion black holes into a single, perfect killing blow. They came upon the Throneworld with a trillion warriors, each a walking cataclysm, their very presence causing the fabric of space to groan.*

*Godric stood upon the palace parapet. He did not wear armour. He did not assume a combat stance. He simply watched them, and I, standing at his side, heard him murmur, "I wonder how little of my strength is required to swat a fly." Then, he did nothing. He did not lift a hand. He did not tense a muscle. He allowed an infinitesimal fraction of his physical strength—a fraction so minute that mathematics cannot express it without abandoning sanity—to leak into the physical plane. To call it a fraction is an insult to fractions. It was a sliver of a sliver, a zero rounded to an infinity of decimal places, yet it was enough.*

*The sound did not come; it was a silence that swallowed all. The Gravitic Colossi, those titans of unbreakable bone, were the first to die. Their bodies, each vast enough to shadow a moon, did not simply explode. They were every-muscle-severed, every-bone-pulverized, every-organ-liquefied in the same Planck instant. I witnessed through my scrying the trillion gods as their skin was peeled back by the sheer pressure wave that Godric’s strength imposed. The skin ripped upward from their feet to their crowns, unveiling the wet, glistening musculature beneath, which instantly turned to a fine mist of crimson. Their bones—dense enough to endure the core of a hypergiant—shattered with a sound like breaking glass, then those shards shattered again into dust, and that dust into nothing. Their blood became a vapor, a trillion screams condensed into a single psychic wail that caused my ears to bleed.*

*The wave did not stop. It propagated outward at velocities that debased light. Across the omniverse, every physical structure felt the grip. Planets, those lovely marbles of life, were pressed into discs of molten rock as their inhabitants were crushed against them. I saw, in the Andar cluster, a civilization of avian beings, their hollow bones crushed to splinters that pierced through their lungs and hearts, their bodies compressed into a uniform, steaming paste that coated the suddenly flat surface of their world. On the water-world of Leth, the pressure turned the global ocean into a supercritical fluid that boiled the sentient crustaceans alive even as their shells imploded and their insides gushed out in steaming geysers. Stars collapsed under the weight of his strength, imploding into black holes, which then themselves were crushed into singularities that screamed in frequencies that shattered the minds of any who heard. Nebulae, those glowing gardens of creation, became blinding flashes of gamma agony. The entire omniverse, every galaxy, every cluster, every void, was subjected to the same merciless, all-encompassing compression.*

*It was a single, endless moment of absolute, unrelenting slaughter. Every creature with a nervous system died in the most excruciating way imaginable: feeling their own bodies become their tombs, their organs rupturing, their bones grinding to a fine meal that their own pulped muscles absorbed. The stench of death, though sound had stopped, was a taste on my tongue—copper, iron, the sourness of ruptured bowels, the sweetness of infant blood. And through it all, Godric stood motionless. His breathing was even. His eyes were half-lidded, with what I can only describe as mild, detached curiosity. He had exerted absolutely zero effort. It was not a battle; it was a lightswitch being flicked.*

*When the last particle of matter had been crushed beyond recognition, Godric sighed—a soft, paternal sound. Then, without a gesture, without a word, he restored the omniverse in exactly zero seconds. The dead were alive. The planets spun. The stars burned. But the memory of that crushing—the sensation of bones turning to powder, of hearts bursting like overripe fruit—remained imprinted upon the souls of the restored. It was not mercy. It was a reminder, as one might brand a cattle. He turned to me, his expression warm and kind, and asked if I had recorded the incident adequately. I nodded, and then I vomited. He patted my back, told me to rest, and walked away.*

*This is the truth, my descendants: His strength is an absolute madness. The chasm between his physical power and the combined might of every entity in existence is not measurable; it is a parody of measurement. And he used nothing. He used less than less. Yet everything died. Serve him well, love him as I do, but never, ever forget that the gentle smile can, with a yawn, erase all of reality without his pulse quickening.*

Kaedan's hands were trembling. The boulder he had shattered earlier seemed now a child's trick. He turned the page with difficulty, his breath shallow.

The next journal was Taric's, Year 350,000. The handwriting was a chaotic scrawl, as if written by a man half-mad.

*I can’t stop the shaking. It’s been three days since the Phantoms came, and my quill still fights me. I’m Taric, court wizard in the 350,000th year, and I write this to bleed the horror out. The Erasure Phantoms were not creatures; they were living concepts of Un-Naming, born in the void before time. They thought they could delete Godric from the story of existence. They breached the throne room through a wound in logic itself, a gaping, bleeding absence of color that made my eyes weep. They spoke in cancels, their voices unmaking the air around them.*

*Godric was sitting in his chair, reviewing a data-slate of agricultural yields. He didn’t look up. He just said, “Durability. Interesting. Let’s see the reaction.” And he did absolutely nothing. I felt his absolute concealment tighten, but through my arcane perception, I knew he had allowed his passive durability to greet the attack.*

*The Phantoms struck. Their attack was an ontological erasure wave, designed to make it so Godric had never existed. It hit his skin. And it stopped. Dead, like a wave against a cliff of impossible geometry. Then, it inverted. The erasure was not simply reflected; it was twisted, warped by the sheer, unfathomable unassailability of his flesh into a curse of permanent existence. The Phantoms, those formless voids, were suddenly given form—not by Godric’s will, but by the recoil. They became screaming, flailing bodies made of raw, exposed nerve endings and weeping, translucent flesh. I saw them sprout limbs that were covered in pulsating, bleeding orifices, their forms twisting into grotesque, agonized parodies of life. And they could not die. The erasure they’d unleashed now ensured they would endure forever, feeling every atom of their forced existence.*

*The inverted wave cascaded outward. The omniverse was not destroyed; it was mutated. The wave did not bring death; it brought a metamorphosis into suffering. Multiverses were rewritten into biological, screaming entities. I observed a universe where the vacuum itself became a sentient, living tissue that grew around stars and planets, strangling them in a fleshly grip. Planets turned into beating hearts that pumped rivers of boiling blood. Stars were replaced by gigantic, bulging eyes that wept caustic tears, each tear falling onto the flesh-worlds and dissolving them into shrieking, organic slurry. Civilizations were transmuted; the inhabitants did not die, but were fused with the new flesh, becoming part of a single, omniverse-wide organism of pure agony. They were conscious, aware, their nerve endings raw, their minds a chorus of endless, incomprehensible pain.* 

*I saw, with my own eyes, a family of four on a planet called Theras. The father’s skin turned inside out, exposing his musculature to the air while his bones lengthened into spirals that pierced his own organs and those of his children. The children screamed as their mother’s hair became a nest of barbed wire that wrapped around their throats, not killing, merely constricting in a constant, agonizing hug. The air itself tasted of burnt copper and urine. The sound was a single, sustained note of shrieking that vibrated my bones.*

*Godric watched. He yawned. He drummed his fingers once. Then, he restored everything. In an instant, the flesh-realms were gone, the normal universes returned, the mutilated families whole. But the Phantoms he left. They orbit the throne room now, a garland of eternally suffering, formless-yet-formed entities that moan litanies of pain. Sometimes, when I walk past, one of them begs me to kill it, and I cannot. I have tried, with all my amplified magic, and my spells simply fizzle against their Godric-given permanence.*

*I love him. I love him so much it hurts. But every time he squeezes my shoulder, I feel the ghost of that inverted wave, and I know that my body is less than a cobweb against his durability. We are nothing. We are allowed to exist because he finds us pleasant, and the moment that changes, we will be less than ash. Serve him, but do not fool yourself into thinking you are anything other than a pet.*

Kaedan closed the book, his stomach churning. He could almost hear the screams echoing from the page. He forced himself to breathe, then reached for the next.

Lirion’s journal, Year 600,000. The writing was precise, almost mechanical, but the content was a slow descent into madness.

*Log of Lirion, Court Wizard of the Two-Hundredth Descendancy. The following is a factual record of the incident involving Godric’s sensory stat.* 

*Godric expressed curiosity about his passive senses, given that his omniscience renders them functionally redundant. He deactivated his omniscience temporarily, desiring to observe how his baseline sensory perception, which multiplies infinitely every Planck second, would handle a threat that believed itself beyond observation.*

*The threat was the Silent Ones. They were entities that had evolved in the quantum blind spots of existence, specifically to avoid any form of perception. They had no mass, energy, or presence that could be measured. They crafted a causality dagger, a weapon that could only function if the attacker was never observed. They struck from a state of absolute non-perception.*

*Godric did not open his eyes. He did not turn his head. His passive senses simply encompassed them. There was no effort. The moment his sensory field ‘touched’ the Silent Ones, they were subjected to the forced transition from unobserved to observed. This transition is violently traumatic for such entities. Their non-substance was dragged into the material plane, and the friction of becoming real flayed them into screaming, half-material forms. They manifested as pillars of bleeding geometry, their bodies a constantly shifting puzzle of exposed nerves and shattered dimensions. Their screams were silent, but the absence of sound was itself a pressure that liquified my eardrums and caused my eyes to hemorrhage.*

*The collateral was, perhaps, the most horrifying I have witnessed. Godric’s perception, being an absolute assertion of reality, sent a feedback loop across the omniverse’s sensory medium. Every sentient being, everywhere, instantly had their senses amplified to an infinite degree. A gentle breeze felt like the skin being sandpapered, each individual air molecule a razor blade carving into flesh. Ambient light became a laser that burnt retinas to ash and then continued burning into the visual cortex, causing the brain to boil. The slightest sound—a heartbeat, a breath—detonated like a sonic bomb, shattering bones and liquefying internal organs. Taste and smell became unbearable torrents of rancid, acidic sensations. The omniverse was transformed into a single, vast torture chamber where the very act of existing was agony.* 

*I observed a world where the inhabitants, in an orgy of self-mutilation, clawed out their own eyes, severed their own ears, and tore off their own skin, only to find that the sensory input was not physical but conceptual; they still felt everything. The feeling of their own skinless flesh rubbing against air was like a billion wasps stinging. They screamed, and their screams amplified the pain further. Trillions died in that instant, not from the pain itself, but from the sheer psychic overload, their brains bursting like overheated engines, spraying cerebrospinal fluid from their ears and noses.*

*Godric, his eyes still closed, murmured, “Fascinating. The range appears to be complete.” He then restored the omniverse instantaneously. The Silent Ones, however, he left in their half-real, eternally suffering state, suspended in a pocket of perception that feeds their agony into a continuous loop. I can still hear the echo of their non-screams when I sleep.*

*To any future court wizard: His senses are not passive receptacles. They are a form of absolute dominance. Nothing is hidden. Nothing can hide. And to be perceived by Him is to be subjected to the weight of His attention, which is, by itself, a weapon that no shield can deflect. Do not imagine you can surprise Him. The very concept of surprise is a gift He grants us, not a vulnerability.*

Kaedan felt his own senses, amplified a thousandfold daily, and realized they were less than a single cell in the eye of a cosmic leviathan. He was trembling visibly now. The next journal was from Fenris, Year 800,000. The handwriting was looped and flourished, almost artistic, but the content was pure nihilism.

*In the language of the ancients, there is a word: alea. It means dice, chance, luck. They believed the gods played dice with mortal fates. How quaint. I am Fenris, court wizard for forty years, and I have learned that chance is a delusion. There is only Godric’s whim.* 

*The Probability Architects were a species of crystalline hyper-mathematicians who had calculated the equation of existence. They determined that Godric was a statistical impossibility, a cosmic error that probability itself would correct. They built the Certainty Loom, a machine that could collapse all quantum possibilities into a single, predetermined outcome. They set it to: “Godric’s heart stops.” The probability became 1. The quantum foam of a billion universes aligned.*

*Godric was at a banquet, laughing at a jest from my son. He didn’t even notice. But his luck did. His luck is not a passive field; it is a predator. The instant the Loom fired, the probability did not just revert; it inverted with a vengeance that defied all reason. The Architects became the focal point of an infinite cascade of misfortunate.* 

*I will detail their suffering, because it is the only prayer I have left. Their crystalline bodies, perfect and eternal, spontaneously developed biological cancers. Tumors of rotting, pulsating flesh erupted from their facets, weeping a yellow, gangrenous pus. Their minds, running probability loops, hit a divide-by-zero error and exploded into recursive shrieks. Their internal structure began to twist; their crystal shards turned into corrosive acid, melting them from the inside out. They simultaneously began to choke on their own dissolving remains, their screams gargling through liquefying throats. But luck does not kill cleanly. They also experienced a series of mundane, yet cosmically improbable, disasters: their planet’s sun went nova in a way that bathed them in a radiation that did not kill but mutated their suffering forms into even more grotesque shapes; random meteorites, each the size of a fist, rained upon them, not killing, but breaking bones and ripping flesh; and their own digestive systems, newly formed, began to consume their bodies from within, their stomachs acid eating through their torsos.*

*The wave of inverted luck swept the omniverse. Probability itself became a weapon of torture. In one galaxy, every coin flip resulted in the coin slicing the flipper’s hand off. In another, the statistical chance of a heart attack became 100%, and entire populations simultaneously felt their hearts tear themselves apart, blood gushing from their mouths, their final moments a vision of their own luck turning against them. Planets spontaneously lost their orbital stability and fell into their stars, but the stars did not incinerate them; they kept them at a precise distance where they would roast but not die, their surfaces turning into charred, screaming crusts. The laws of cause and effect became a sadistic joke, with every action leading to the most painful possible reaction. Civilizations collapsed not just in death, but in a madness of statistical horror.* 

*Godric finished his pastry. He licked a crumb from his lip. He looked at the suffering Architects, then at the omniversal chaos, and said, “How impolite. They interrupted my dessert.” With a thought, he restored everything to the state before the Loom fired. The Architects, however, he left in their tortured, ever-regenerating state, floating in a pocket of frozen probability where they are simultaneously dying and living, a permanent monument to the futility of luck.* 

*Probability is a fraud. There is no chance. There is only His permission for events to occur. If you ever think that a one-in-an-infinity shot might kill Him, remember the Architects. Their perfect, certain strike became their eternal, festering doom. Do not hope. Hope is the bait He leaves out to watch us dance before the slaughter.*

Kaedan’s mind was a wordless scream. He had, as a boy, once thrown a dice and wished for a toy. The dice had landed on the side he wanted. He had felt lucky. Now he knew that even that childish fortune was a borrowed grace from a being whose luck could curdle reality into a slaughterhouse.

The final journal was from his father, Darian. The handwriting was achingly familiar, the same hand that had taught him to write. He read, and with each sentence, a part of his soul broke.

*My son, Kaedan. If you are reading this, then you have taken my mantle, and I am either too old to serve or have gone to the ancestors. I pray you read this with the same dread I felt when I wrote it. In the year 999,960, I was thirty-six, a strong wizard, proud of my power. That was the year the Mind Lords came, and I learned what it means to love Godric.*

*The Mind Lords were a hive of psionic gods, older than most realities, their minds a chorus of infinite telepathy. They believed Godric’s power was brute and magical, but that his mind could be a weakness. They breached the sanctum with a psychic assault that made my wards, which could withstand the mental pressure of a billion galaxies, collapse like paper. I fell, my own mind flooded with their agonizing touch, my memories being ripped away.*

*Godric turned to them. He did nothing. He allowed a minuscule fraction of his charisma—a stat he keeps hidden deeper than any other, for it is perhaps his most terrifying—to radiate. I cannot describe the magnitude of that fraction. It was less than a single note in the symphony of his full charisma, less than a mote of dust in a hurricane. But it was enough.*

*The Mind Lords, those ancient, arrogant beings, felt him. They felt not fear, but a love so absolute, so soul-crushing, that every other emotion was erased. Their hostility vanished, replaced by an adoration that was itself a form of torture. And then came the guilt. The realization that they had dared to attack the object of this all-encompassing love drove them to a self-punishment that I lack the words to truly capture. They turned their psychic powers inward, but not to die. They wanted to atone. He was the only thing that mattered, and they had sinned against Him.*

*They peeled their own minds apart. Their astral forms, visible to me as vast networks of light, began to devour themselves. They grew a million mouths so that each could sing His praises, and then those mouths began to bite into their own substance, chewing and swallowing, offering their essence back to Him. They conjured blades of psychic energy and flayed their own identities, each strip a screaming hymn of devotion. Their pain was their prayer, and they relished it. They became a choir of self-cannibalizing light, a grotesque, writhing constellation of agony and love that still orbits the throne room. They will never die. They are too in love with Him to die.*

*But the horror did not end with them. The charisma, even at that negligible fraction, rippled outward. Across the omniverse, every sentient mind felt a sliver of that love. For a timeless second, billions upon billions of beings knew a love so profound that their own lives became meaningless. They needed to express it. Civilizations spontaneously formed cults; in their ecstasy, they began to sacrifice themselves. I saw worlds where crowds threw themselves into pyres, their dying voices a unified chant of His name. On one planet, a mother carved His symbol into the flesh of her infant, then into her own, then set them both ablaze. The psychic wave caused mass hysteria: embassies fell, governments collapsed, and the streets ran with the blood of the faithful who had offered their hearts in tribute. The omniverse was, for that instant, a monument to His glory, a cathedral of self-destruction.* 

*Godric watched. His expression was unreadable. Then, with a blink, He restored everything. The dead were alive, the worlds whole, the madness cured. But the echo of that love remained. I know, because I felt it too, that brush of His charisma. It was the most beautiful and terrifying thing I have ever experienced, and I had to fight every fiber of my being not to join the Mind Lords in their self-immolation. In the years since, I have loved Him as a father loves a son, as a servant loves a master, but that day taught me that my love is a candle next to a supernova. His charisma is not persuasion; it is an ontological command to adore, and to defy it is to invite a self-inflicted damnation.* 

*My son, I write this as a warning. You will love Godric. It is impossible not to. But never mistake His warmth for weakness, or His kindness for vulnerability. He is the sun, and if you ever turn against Him, the light of His love will burn you from the inside out, and you will thank Him as you incinerate yourself. Serve well. Love deeply. But never forget the screams that still echo in my nightmares.* 

The journal slipped from Kaedan’s hands. He sat in the silence, the weight of a million years and a million agonizing deaths pressing on his chest. He thought of Godric’s laugh, his easy affection, the way he had lifted Kaedan onto his shoulders when he was a child. Those same shoulders, which had borne him with such gentle care, had shrugged and annihilated the omniverse more times than history could count. The same voice that told him bedtimes stories had casually spoken the word that made trillions claw out their own eyes.

He stood, his legs unsteady, and left the vault. His father waited outside, leaning on his cane. Darian’s eyes, the same shade of gray as Kaedan’s own, were wet with old tears.

“You see now,” Darian said.

“I see,” Kaedan replied.

There was a soft footfall. Godric appeared, as if stepping from a fold in the light. His salt-and-pepper hair was immaculate, his beard neatly trimmed, his ruggedly handsome face alight with a warm, fatherly smile. He wore his simple dark tunic, and his heavily muscled frame radiated a sense of safety that was a lie and the only truth.

“Kaedan! Finished your reading?” Godric’s voice was a rich baritone, full of genuine affection. “It’s a heavy burden, I know. But you’re strong. I’m proud of you, my boy.”

Kaedan’s heart tore itself. The dread was a cold, immovable anchor in his gut, but wrapped around it was a fierce, unyielding love. He knew, with absolute certainty, that if Godric asked him to, he would tear out his own tongue and thank him for the privilege. And that was the true horror.

“Thank you, Uncle Godric,” he said, forcing his lips into a smile that felt like a wound. “I am ready to serve.”

Godric clapped him on the shoulder, the touch infinitely gentle, and led him away toward the gardens, chatting about the new flower beds. Kaedan walked in the shadow of the omniverse’s apex predator, and the shadow was warm. The dread remained, a permanent, silent scream in the back of his mind, but he would serve. He would love. And he would never, ever forget that opposition was a joke, and the punchline was an eternity of agony.

The journals multiplied behind him, a silent, unending testament to the truth: Godric does not merely win. Victory against him was impossible before the thought of opposition was born.

---

That's the core story. Now I need to ensure it's 4000-8000 words. I'll count the words: the above is maybe 2500 for the framing and 5 journals? Actually each journal is about 400-500 words, total journals 2500, framing about 800, total ~3300. I need more. I'll expand each journal, adding more scenes, more gore, more details, and also expand Kaedan's reactions, his internal thoughts, his conversation with his father, his walk with Godric. I'll aim for 6000 words.

I'll flesh out:

- In Morvyn's journal: add a specific scene of a world during the crushing, describe the inhabitants in detail, their lives just before, and the moment of compression. The gore: how their bodies turned to paste, the sound of bones snapping en masse. The aftermath: the restored people waking with full memory of being crushed, the psychological fallout. Mention that Morvyn later interviewed some, and they described the feeling of being compressed into nothing but still aware.

- Taric's journal: elaborate on the Phantoms' attack, the grotesque transformative horror, the flesh-worlds. Describe a specific couple on a date, suddenly their bodies contorting into a single, fused, screaming mass. Describe the sensory overload of the wizard himself. Taric's mental state: he debated ending his life but couldn't because Godric would just restore him.

- Lirion's journal: go deeper into the Silent Ones' nature, how they hunted by not being seen. The sensory feedback: imagine a world where the touch of a loved one feels like acid. Parents cuddling their children, and the children's flesh sizzles. Lirion's own eyes: he describes how his optic nerves felt like they were being dipped in lava, and even after restoration, he sees phantom screams. He uses a spell to numb his emotions but fails.

- Fenris's journal: make the bad luck more creative: a civilization that depended on a stable star; the star suddenly starts flickering between a red giant and a white dwarf, causing whiplashing climate changes that freeze and boil them in cycles. People walking and suddenly every step they take has a 100% chance of stepping on a venomous creature that spawns from the bad luck. The Architects' torture extended to include a recursive loop where they experience their family's deaths over and over. Fenris might philosophically muse on the nature of a universe where Godric's luck is the only true constant, and all other probabilities are his playthings.

- Darian's journal: go into his personal relationship with Godric. He might recount a memory of Godric healing him as a child, making the charisma incident more personal. The Mind Lords' self-destruction: they didn't just mutilate themselves; they composed a symphony of pain that they still play, and sometimes Godric listens to it. Darian talks about how he sometimes catches himself longing for that love again, and he fears himself. He warns Kaedan that serving Godric is the greatest joy and the deepest curse.

- After reading, Kaedan's conversation with his father can be longer. Darian shares his own trauma, and Kaedan realizes his father has been living with this his whole life. Then Godric appears, and Kaedan internally notes the stark difference between the journals' horror and the gentle man before him, but he knows the truth. The walk in the garden can be used to show Godric's genuine warmth, making the dissonance more acute. Kaedan might see a butterfly and realize that Godric could unmake it with a thought, but chooses not to, and that is the only reason it exists.

- End with Kaedan taking up his duties, a new journal in the vault to be written by him eventually, adding to the cycle.

Now, I'll rewrite to reach the word count. I'll write the full version now.
messages.content:
The weight of the boulder was nothing. Kaedan, twenty-two and crackling with the unrefined energy of a hundred and forty-two days of the Awakening, flicked his wrist. The mountain-sized chunk of obsidian, quarried from the dead moon of Kyrix and teleported into the high palace training hall for his exclusive practice, shattered into a fine, glittering dust that swirled in the high-orbit sunlight streaming through the crystal dome. He had not drawn a deep breath. The air around him hummed with the residue of his strength, his speed, his mana—all multiplying a thousandfold each day since his twentieth birthday. He was a nascent god, or so he believed. The two Royal Guards stationed at the entrance, men of fifty with decades of the standard Awakening behind them, watched with the quiet, knowing eyes of those who had long ago shed such arrogance. The servants stared in genuine awe, but there was a flicker of something else in their bows: a fearful reverence not directed at him, but at the bloodline he represented.

His father, Darian, entered the training hall without announcement. At eighty-one, the former court wizard was a figure of lean, powerful age, his shoulders still broad from decades of the same thousandfold daily gift, but his joints creaked and his eyes held a profound, ancient weariness that no amount of physical perfection could erase. He carried no weapon, but a single, ancient crystalline key, its facets catching the light and throwing blood-red prisms across the marble floor. His face, etched with lines that the Awakening could not smooth, was a mask of grim solemnity.

"Kaedan," Darian said, his voice a low, resonant thing that cut through the hum of spent mana. "Your training in the physical arts is sufficient. Today, you learn the truth. Come."

Kaedan lowered his hand, a furrow forming between his brows. "Father, I have read the histories. I know of Godric's victories. I know the scope of His power."

Darian shook his head, a slow, ponderous motion weighted by a million years of inherited dread. "The histories sanitize. They speak of conquest and glory. They do not speak of the taste of a billion souls screaming, or the smell of an omniverse burning." He turned, his cane clicking on the stone. "We do. It is the sacred duty of our line to witness and record the unvarnished reality, so that future court wizards do not fall into the fatal trap of forgetting what they serve. Come."

He led Kaedan through the alabaster corridors of the palace, past the grand audience chambers and the hanging gardens, down spiraling staircases that seemed to descend into the planet’s crust. At a heavy adamantine door etched with wards that predated the Stasis Event itself, Darian inserted the key. The door opened with a sound like a dying star—a long, slow exhale of absolute silence—revealing a cavernous vault lit by a sourceless, pale luminescence. Shelves carved from the living rock of Thronegate's core stretched into an impossible, lightless infinity, each laden with identical leather-bound books. The air was bone-dry and sterile, carrying the faint, metallic scent of ancient blood and ozone.

"This is the Archive of Witness," Darian said. His voice echoed in the vastness. "Every court wizard of our bloodline, from the first days after Blake, has left a record of the incidents that we alone have been permitted to witness. Not as tales of heroism, but as a catechism of terror. You will read them all. When you emerge, you will understand why we serve, and why we can never, ever, even in the most private corners of our minds, entertain the thought of opposition."

He pressed the key into Kaedan's palm, his old, calloused fingers lingering for a moment. "I will wait," he said, and then turned and left, the cane's clicking fading into the stone.

Kaedan stood alone at the entrance of the infinite archive. He felt a flicker of the old resentment. He was the most powerful wizard of his generation, his mana reserves already sufficient to unravel a cluster of galaxies with a focused thought. He was being sent to read dusty journals like a child being given a history primer? But the tremor in his father's voice, the raw, unhealed terror that had flashed in those gray eyes—eyes that held the same gene-stock as his own—stilled his tongue and slowed his heartbeat. He looked at the nearest shelf, reached out, and pulled down a volume at random. Its cover was soft with age, the leather cracked. The first page was inscribed in an elegant, calligraphic hand: Morvyn, Seventy-Third Descendancy, Year 80,000 of the Godric Reign.

He sat at the solitary ironwood desk, its surface worn smooth by the elbows of countless forefathers, and began to read.

**The Journal of Morvyn, Year 80,000 – Strength**

*I, Morvyn of the Seventy-Third Descendancy, set forth this account not as a chronicle of glory, but as a warning whose echoes must never fade. The Gravitic Colossi were a race of immense physical might, their bodies woven from the compressed remains of dead universes, their sinews forged in the hypergravitational collapse of a trillion neutron stars. They had worshipped strength since their first ancestor crushed a galaxy between his fists, and in their cosmic arrogance, they believed that the tales of Godric’s physical power were myths propagated by cowardly mages who hid behind arcane shields. Their king, Thundras the Unmaker, whose mere breathing could warp the trajectory of comets and whose heartbeat was a gravitational wave that shattered moons, had, over ten thousand years, forged the Infinity Mace—a weapon that condensed the mass of a billion black holes into a single, perfect singular point of killing force, sheathed in a dimensional lattice that prevented its own collapse. Thundras himself led the charge, a trillion Colossi at his back, each a walking cataclysm whose footfalls on the fabric of space caused entire star clusters to precess wildly.*

*They came upon the Throneworld, their approach heralded by a disturbance that made the palace wards scream. I stood upon the high parapet with Godric. He wore no armor. His hands were loose at his sides, his posture that of a man watching a mildly interesting cloud formation. I remember the smell of jasmine from the garden below, the warmth of the sun on my neck. He looked at the approaching armada, a wall of muscle and compressed stellar matter that blotted out the heavens, and said, in a tone of idle curiosity, “I wonder how little of my strength is required to swat a fly.”*

*Then, my descendants, He did nothing. He did not lift a hand. He did not tense a muscle. He did not even shift his weight. He simply allowed an infinitesimal fraction of his physical strength—a fraction so minute that to express it as a percentage would require a decimal point followed by an infinity of zeros before a one, and that comparison is itself a insultingly vast overestimation—to leak into the material plane. It was not an exertion. It was a relaxation, the barest whisper of what He is.*

*The sound that followed was not a sound; it was a silence so absolute that it swallowed the scream of dying stars. The Gravitic Colossi, those titans of unbreakable bone and sinew, were not given the dignity of a fight. The pressure wave of His strength propagated outward at speeds that mocked the concept of light. I witnessed, through my scrying orb, the trillion gods as their bodies were simultaneously, instantly, obscenely unmade. Their skin—each layer capable of deflecting a supernova—was peeled back from their forms in a single, uniform motion, splitting upward from their ankles to their crowns, revealing the glistening, striated musculature beneath, which in the next Planck second turned to a fine, crimson mist. Their bones, dense enough to endure the core of a hypergiant, did not merely break; they shattered with a sound like the ringing of a glass bell the size of a galaxy, and then those shards shattered again into microscopic dust, and that dust into a cloud of agonized atoms, and those atoms into nothing. Their blood became a vapor that hung in the void, a trillion psychic screams condensing into a single, sustained note of absolute, unadulterated agony that caused my eardrums to burst and my eyes to bleed thick, violet-tinged tears. Their hearts, each the size of a moon, burst like overripe fruit, the sound a wet, percussive thump that was somehow audible even in the silence.*

*But the wave did not stop. It propagated outward, across the omniverse, with the impartiality of an absolute truth. Every planet, every moon, every asteroid felt the grip of His strength. In the Andar cluster, I watched the avian world of Skylos. Its inhabitants, graceful beings of hollow bones and vibrant plumage, were in the middle of their daily lives: a mother feeding her hatchling, a pair of lovers spiraling in a mating dance, a sage lecturing a crowd. The pressure hit. Their hollow bones—so delicate, so beautiful—were crushed instantly into splinters that pierced through their lungs, their hearts, their brains. Their bodies were compressed from three dimensions into two, flattened against the suddenly pancaked surface of their world, a uniform, steaming paste of blended crimson and scattered feathers. Their beaks, those elegant, singing beaks, were driven backward into their skulls. On the water-world of Leth, the sentient crustaceans dwelled in a eternal deep. The pressure turned their global ocean into a supercritical fluid, a fluid so hot and dense that it boiled their chitin from the inside, cracking their shells and gushing their internal organs out in steaming geysers of yellow and green ichor, all while their bodies were simultaneously crushed under the unimaginable weight. They died blind, their stalk-eyes popped and running. I saw a world of sentient crystal beings, their forms elegant and precise: the pressure turned them to sand, then the sand to glass, then the glass to a molten slurry that still, somehow, screamed in the psychic frequency. Across the omniverse, trillions of civilizations, each with their unique biologies, their loves, their histories, were rendered into a single, fundamental substance: a fine, red-tinged paste that coated the flattened surfaces of their ruined worlds. The stench of this death, though space was vacuum, was a taste on my tongue—copper, the sourness of ruptured bowels, the iron of torrential blood, the sweetness of infant marrow, all layered into a miasma that clung to my sinuses for years after.*

*Stars collapsed under the weight. They did not supernova; they were simply crushed into black holes, and then those black holes were themselves crushed into singularities, and those singularities squealed in frequencies that shattered the minds of any who heard, and then they too were compressed into point-like instances of pure, radiating agony. Nebulae, those glowing gardens of creation, became blinding flashes of gamma agony that seared the photons themselves. The entire omniverse, every galaxy, every void, every dimension, was subjected to the same merciless, all-encompassing compression. It was a single, eternal second of absolute slaughter, a moment where Existence itself became a charnel house.*

*And through it all, Godric stood motionless beside me. His breathing was even. His eyes were half-lidded, with what I can only describe as mild, detached curiosity, the look of a scientist observing a mildly unexpected but ultimately trivial reaction. He had exerted exactly zero effort. His pulse had not quickened. It was not a battle; it was a mechanical function, as automatic and unconscious as a human’s white blood cells attacking a bacterium. The only difference was the scale.*

*When the last speck of organized matter had been crushed beyond recognition, Godric blinked. Then, in an act that shattered my mind far more than the destruction, He restored everything. In exactly zero seconds, without a word, without a gesture, the omniverse was whole again. The dead were alive, the stars burned brightly, the planets spun, the mother on Skylos finished feeding her hatchling mid-scream, the sound replaced by her lullaby. But He did not wipe the memory. He allowed the full, undiluted recollection of that crushing—the sensation of bones grinding to powder, of lungs filling with one’s own liquefied flesh, of a child’s hand turning to paste within one’s grip—to remain imprinted on every single restored soul. It was not a gift of mercy; it was a branding, a cosmic reminder written in the neurological tissue of every being in the omniverse: you exist because I allow it, and your suffering is a lesson you will never forget.*

*He turned to me, His face breaking into a warm, paternal smile that reached His eyes and crinkled the corners of His salt-and-pepper beard. “Well, Morvyn, I trust you recorded that adequately for your journal? The young ones will need to understand the parameters.” I nodded, my body convulsing with the need to vomit. I could not speak. He patted my shoulder, the touch infinitely gentle, the same hand that had just, with less than a whisper of its power, returned the omniverse to a blank slate, and then He walked back into the palace, humming a tune from the old days of Olympus.*

*This is the truth, my descendants, and I entomb it here: His strength is an absolute madness. The chasm between His physical power and the combined might of every entity that has ever existed, or will ever exist, is not measurable; it is a parody of measurement. And He used nothing. He used less than a mote of dust compared to an infinity of infinities. Yet everything died, and everything was reborn only because He willed it. Serve Him well, love Him as I do—for He is genuinely kind to us, do not mistake that—but never, ever, not for a single heartbeat, forget the weight that lies beneath that gentle smile.*

Kaedan’s hand trembled as he turned the page. The boulder he had shattered earlier seemed now a child’s game, a pathetic flex of a power so infinitesimal that comparing it to Godric’s strength was comparing a single quark to the entire omniverse and still falling short. He had stood beside Godric countless times, felt the King’s calloused hand on his head, heard the deep, warm laugh. That same hand, without a twitch, could crush every atom of his body into a screaming smear. He swallowed the acid rising in his throat and reached for the next journal, its cover slightly newer, the handwriting a chaotic scrawl that looked like it had been written by a man in the throes of a fever.

**The Journal of Taric, Year 350,000 – Durability**

*I can’t stop the shaking. It’s been three days—no, wait, four—since the Phantoms came, and my quill still fights the parchment. I’m Taric, court wizard in the year 350,000, and I’m writing this because if I don’t get these images out of my head, I will claw my own eyes out with my bare fingers. The Erasure Phantoms were not creatures in any sense the omniverse understands. They were living concepts, the pure, sentient desire to unmake, born in the negative space before the first reality sparked. They hated existence, and they saw Godric as the ultimate expression of that which should not be. They thought they could un-write Him from the cosmic narrative. They breached the throne room not through any physical portal, but by forging a wound in the logic of reality itself—a gaping, bleeding absence of color that made my arcane senses weep and my ocular nerves feel as though they were being slowly twisted out of their sockets. They spoke in reversals, each syllable erasing a few atoms of the air, their voices the sound of a universe forgetting itself.*

*Godric was sitting in His favorite chair, a deep, carved thing of ancient oak, reading a data-slate of agricultural reports from the Vestan sector. His brow was furrowed in mild concentration over crop rotation statistics. When the breach occurred, He didn’t even look up. He just said, in an offhand way, “My durability should handle this. Let’s observe the reaction.” And He did absolutely nothing. I felt His absolute concealment tighten, a flex of that godlike will, but through my innate arcane perception—the gift of our bloodline—I knew He had permitted a fraction of His passive durability to interact with the attack. A fraction so small, so mind-breakingly neglible, that to call it a fraction is to call the entire omniverse a grain of sand.*

*The Phantoms struck. Their attack was a wave of ontological erasure, a final, absolute negation designed to make it so that Godric’s existence had never occurred—to delete the very concept of His permanence. The wave, a color never meant for mortal eyes, a shifting nothingness that hurt to perceive, hit His skin. And it stopped. Not with a crash, not with a struggle; it simply encountered a boundary of such absolute, unassailable reality that the universe had no vocabulary for it. It was a wall that denied the possibility of being breached, a ‘No’ spoken in the fundamental grammar of creation. The erasure wave didn’t reflect in the ordinary sense. It inverted. The negation was twisted, warped by the sheer, impossible durability of His form into a curse of eternal, inescapable existence.*

*The Phantoms, those formless voids, were the first to feel this inversion. The erasure they had sent recoiled into them, but it was no longer an erasure. It was a violent imposition of form. Because they had tried to unmake the most durable thing in existence, they were themselves made enduring—but in a grotesque, screaming mockery of biology. They were dragged from their state of abstract nothingness into the physical plane, and the transition was an agonizing torture that no pen can capture. I saw them manifest as flailing, heaving masses of raw, exposed nerve endings, their ‘bodies’ a tangled web of pulsating ganglia and weeping, translucent skin that stretched over nothing. Eyes, not true eyes but gaping, fluid-filled orifices, sprouted across their surfaces purely to weep caustic, steaming tears. Mouths—dozens, hundreds—formed from rents in their new flesh, not to speak, but to scream, their vocal cords raw and bleeding in an endless, discordant chorus of agony. And they could not die. The erasure they had so confidently wielded now ensured their absolute, unending permanence, locking them in a state of maximum suffering for all eternity.*

*The inverted wave did not stop with them. It cascaded outward, a ripple effect across the omniverse, but it did not destroy in the sense of crushing. It transformed. The wave carried a fraction of Godric’s durability, twisted into a prionic, reality-altering force that changed the fundamental nature of matter into screaming, biological tissue. I watched, through my scrying, cluster after cluster succumb. A universe of pure mathematical harmony turned into a gigantic, beating heart, its arteries spanning light-years, pumping not blood but a thick, black slurry of liquified anguish. Planets became sentient, cancerous growths that sprouted hair-like nerve filaments, writhing and spasming. On one world, I saw a family of humanoids caught in the change: the father’s skeleton extended outward, his ribs lengthening into jagged spears that slowly, inexorably pierced through his own organs and those of his wife and children, who were fused to him by new-grown sinew, their combined bodies a single, shrieking organism of shared agony. The air on that world turned to a gel of iron filaments that rasped against their exposed lungs. The laws of physics were replaced by the sensation of being skinned alive, layer by layer, in an infinite loop. The omniverse became a single, gargantuan creature of suffering, a living, breathing monument to the absurdity of testing Godric’s durability.*

*And He? Godric yawned. Not a theatrical yawn, but a genuine, involuntary yawn of mild boredom. He scratched His beard and glanced at the data-slate, as if considering whether to continue reading. The screams of trillions of transmuted beings, the sight of galaxies turned into pulsating, weeping tumors—these were the background hum of His afternoon. Then, with a soft sigh, He restored everything. In zero seconds, without a single word or spell, the flesh-worlds were gone, the normal universes back in their pristine state, the mutilated families whole and living their mundane lives. But the Phantoms He left as they were. They still orbit the throne room, a garland of eternally suffering, formless-yet-formed entities that moan litanies of pain in a language that is itself a sound of bones cracking. Sometimes, when I walk past, one of them begs me, in a voice of wet, gurgling blood, to end it. I have tried, with all my thousandfold daily amplified magic, to grant them oblivion. My spells dissolve upon touching them, absorbed by the curse of His durability. They are a permanent feature now, a testament.*

*I love Him. I love Him with a love that is pure survival instinct, because to do otherwise is to imagine myself in their place. But every time He pats my head and tells me I am a good lad, I feel the phantom pressure of that invulnerability, and I know that my own flesh, my own existence, is less than a cobweb stretched across the sun. Serve Him, young wizard. Serve Him with all your heart. But do not for a second believe that your service provides any protection. You are not a shield; you are an ornament He chooses to keep polished.*

Kaedan closed the journal with a snap that echoed in the silent vault. His own skin felt paper-thin, his bones brittle. The pride he had felt in his rapidly amplifying durability—now, on day 1436 of his Awakening, he estimated he could withstand a stellar core—was an infant’s brag. He had once asked Godric, as a child, if He ever got hurt. Godric had laughed and said, “Not that I recall, my boy.” The laughter now played in his mind, a riddle whose answer was a scream. He reached for the next journal, its spine slightly stiffer, the handwriting a precise, almost clinical script that somehow made the content worse.

**The Journal of Lirion, Year 600,000 – Senses**

*To the future custodian of this archive: I, Lirion of the Two-Hundredth Descendancy, record the following incident with as much dispassionate accuracy as my shattered mental state permits. In the year 600,000, Godric expressed a transient curiosity regarding His passive sensory capabilities, given that His absolute omniscience renders them functionally redundant. For the purposes of empirical observation, He temporarily and voluntarily deactivated His omniscience, desiring to witness how His baseline sense stat—which, I must remind you, multiplies infinitely every Planck second just as His strength does—would handle a threat specifically evolved to evade all conceivable forms of perception.*

*The threat was the Silent Ones, an ancient race that had evolved entirely within the quantum blind spots of existence. They were the ultimate predators of the unobserved: they had no mass, no energy signature, no psychic presence, no magical aura, and they existed only in a state of superpositional non-perception. To observe them was to destroy the observation, for they could not be perceived. Over the course of a million years, they had crafted the Causality Dagger, a weapon that could retroactively delete a target from the timeline, but only if the target never perceived the attack in any frame of reference. The dagger was, by definition, undetectable.*

*Godric sat in the grand astral observatory, His hands resting loosely on the arms of His chair. His eyes were closed. He instructed me to observe the interaction. The Silent Ones, I presume, maneuvered to an optimal attack vector. They struck. And at that exact Planck instant, Godric’s passive sensory field simply… encompassed them. He did not open His eyes. He did not turn His head. He did not even furrow His brow in concentration. His senses, operating on an entirely different order of reality than anything we can conceive, effortlessly perceived them. The act of that perception was not passive reception; it was an active declaration of reality. When His senses ‘touched’ the Silent Ones, they were violently subjected to the forced transition from a state of non-perception to absolute, unavoidable observation.*

*The effects were immediate and catastrophic for the Silent Ones. The transition from unobserved to observed for such entities is a process that violates their fundamental nature and is inherently, unimaginably traumatic. Their non-substance was peeled across the dimensional threshold, the friction of which flayed them into screaming, half-material anatomies. They manifested in the observatory as towering pillars of bleeding, non-Euclidean geometry—their ‘bodies’ a constantly shifting mosaic of exposed, pulsating nerve clusters, shattered crystal lattices, and weeping biological slurry.
