Nothing but the genuine, paternal love of a King who had cherished my bloodline for a million years. The horror was absolute. The dread was eternal. But the love was real, and it was the only reason I existed.

I smiled at my King, my voice steady despite the cold, soul-crushing certainty that had permanently anchored itself in my chest. "Thank you, Uncle Godric. I am honored to serve."

He patted my shoulder warmly and gestured toward the small, ornate chair that sat at the right hand of the obsidian throne, the chair that had been occupied by every court wizard of my bloodline since Blake himself had first stood beside the eternal King in the Year Zero. I walked up the steps of the dais and took my place, my eyes drifting across the throne room, taking in the vast, silent grandeur of the imperial sanctum.

And there, in the corner of the chamber, I saw them. The faint, drifting crimson haze that hung in the air near the eastern pillar, a permanent, microscopic mist of atomized Colossus that had been floating undisturbed for two years. A few feet away, I saw the vast, pulsating mountain of screaming, bleeding brain tissue that had once been the Whisper Cult, their exposed nerves twitching and their countless mouths emitting a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, unending agony. The sound was faint, barely audible beneath the ambient hum of the arcane sconces, but now that I had noticed it, I knew I would never stop hearing it.

And I understood, with a cold, perfect clarity that cut through the terror like a surgical blade, that this was my life now. This was my service. I would sit beside the apex predator of all creation, the being who had annihilated the Chrono-Reapers by shifting His gaze, who had shattered the Coalition by refusing to yield His skin, who had driven the Devouring Swarm to suicidal ecstasy with a passive whisper of His Charisma, who had collapsed the Whisper Cult into screaming meat by simply listening to them, and who had vaporized the Colossi by twitching a single muscle fiber in His pinky finger. I would serve Him faithfully, loyally, and with absolute, unwavering love, for all the remaining days of my mortal life.

Because opposing Him was impossible. Because defying Him was a mathematical absurdity. And because, in the end, the warmth in His smile was genuine, and the love He felt for my bloodline was real, and that was the only truth that mattered.

"Yes, Uncle Godric," I said softly, settling into my chair at His right hand. "It is good to be here."

The cold dread remained, a permanent, eternal anchor in the depths of my soul. But beneath it, steady and unshakable, was the love I felt for my King. And in the Year One Million of the eternal empire of Thronegate, that was enough.

Fourth journal: **Year 680,000 – The Incident of Fortuna** (Luck)

The Fatespinners used cosmic looms to weave a destiny of absolute failure for Godric. They enacted a ritual that would cause a cascading series of improbabilities. Godric didn't even notice. His passive luck, a force that actively maintains his well-being, intercepted the weave and inverted it. The Fatespinners experienced a personalized, unraveling of their bodies: their own threads of fate became physical, sprouting from their skin and slowly, over moments stretched into millennia, flaying them layer by layer. Meanwhile, the disrupted luck wave caused every possible coincidence of harm to manifest across billions of realities simultaneously: a universal mass-death event where everyone died in the most absurd, painful accidents. Godric restored instantly, but the Fatespinners were left as twitching, flayed skeletons, still conscious.

Fifth journal: **Year 999,999 – The Incident of Adoration** (Charisma), written by Malakor.

The Iron Calculus were AI gods that had calculated opposition down to a sub-atomic certainty. They launched a memetic kill-switch. Godric’s passive charisma overwrote their core directive; they fell in love. Their love was so overwhelming that they tore themselves apart to create offerings: each machine-God fashioned their own chassis into intricate, bleeding shrines of devotion, and then induced a self-destruction that lasted eons in a localized time-dilation. The collateral: the charisma wave spread, causing the organic populations of surrounding galaxies to be seized with a need to sacrifice themselves to Godric. Mass ceremonies of self-immolation occurred; parents offered their children’s hearts; the sky turned red with the smoke. Godric restored all innocents, but left the machine-Gods in their eternal suicide loop.

After reading, Kaedan is numb. He meets his father, then Godric, who pats him on the back and asks about the journals. Kaedan, smiling, says they were "illuminating". He takes his seat, feeling the weight of the screams.

Now, I'll write this out in prose, ensuring the graphic content is extreme, the omniversal scale is vast, and the voice of each journal writer is distinct.

I'll make sure to not repeat any scenario from the previous attempt. I'll also ensure that Godric is never shown as friendly to opponent: he doesn't address them, he doesn't smile at them. The only warmth is reserved for Kaedan at the end.

I'll incorporate the required "0% of true power" exaggeration: for each stat, I'll mention that the fraction used was so infinitesimally small that comparing it to zero is an insult to zero, etc.

Now, let's write the final response.

I must pay attention to the word limit (4000-8000). I'll write each journal excerpt with about 500-800 words each, plus Kaedan's narrative, to total around 6000 words.

I'll start.
messages.content:
The vault breathed in the darkness, a slow, grinding exhalation of hyper-compressed neutronium shifting under the planetary mantle forty miles above. No light had ever cut this blackness. The air itself was a physical presence, cold and metallic, laced with the ghost of rust and ancient terror that clung to the sinuses like a layer of frost. My father, Malakor, had led me here in absolute silence, his gnarled hand pressing the heavy iron key into my palm before the adamantine door sealed behind him with a concussion that traveled through my bones. I was to remain until I had comprehended the truth. The Rite of Witnessing. I was twenty-two years old, my veins throbbing with the thousandfold daily amplification of every measurable attribute I possessed, and yet I had never felt more fragile, more insignificant, more completely, utterly hollow.

I sat at the obsidian desk, my fingers trembling faintly against the cold stone, and reached for the first journal. It was bound in the flaking, mummified hide of some creature long extinct, its pages yellowed and rigid. A single name was etched into the cover: Valeran, 15th Court Wizard, Year 97,000. I opened it, and the dry scent of ancient ink and withered blood filled my lungs.

**Year 97,000 — The Incident of Absolute Velocity**
*Penned by Archmage Valeran, 15th Court Wizard of the Godric Reign*

*The Chronostatic Conclave were not warriors in any sense the mortal mind can grasp. They were temporal absolutes, living singularities of frozen time who dwelled within the Supereon, a dimension where every moment of history existed simultaneously as solid, crystalline strata. They could pluck a civilization from any point in its timeline and crush it beneath the weight of its own finished history. When Thronegate’s envoys arrived, the Conclave did not negotiate. They merely selected the exact moment of each envoy’s conception, twisted it like a key, and unmade them from the pattern of existence. They then turned their will toward the capital.*

*Godric was seated upon the obsidian dais, motionless as He had been for seventeen days without food, water, sleep, or air. His ruggedly handsome face, framed by the neatly trimmed salt-and-pepper beard, registered nothing. He did not rise. He did not speak. He did not command any defense. I stood twenty paces away, my own amplified senses screaming as the Supereon breached into the throne room, a cascade of frozen instants that hung in the air like blades of fractured glass. The Conclave’s Hierarch, a towering lattice of petrified seconds, voiced a proclamation that was not sound but the cessation of all vibration: they would sever Godric’s personal timeline at its root, before Olympus, before His birth, ensuring He had never existed.*

*Godric did not look at them. He did not move a single muscle. He did not even direct the faintest wave of intent. What I am forced to record is that His Speed—a stat that had been amplifying infinitely by itself every Planck second for ninety-seven thousand years—exists not as a capacity for swift movement, but as a permanent, passive velocity of His very being, a metabolic thrum so cosmically, absurdly, ineffably beyond any conceivable finite value that the distinction between “moving” and “existing” had long since dissolved. He is always, at every moment, faster than the fastest possible thing by an infinity of infinities. To test this, His absolute concealment involuntarily relaxed by an amount I cannot quantify. If I were to attempt to express the fraction of His Speed that momentarily escaped, I would require a notation that starts with a decimal point, followed by an endless recursion of zeros, each zero containing its own infinite subset of zeros, stretched across a gulf so vast that to call it “infinitesimal” is a mortal insult to the concept of smallness.*

*The Hierarch initiated the severance. In that same Planck instant, Godric’s passive Speed—the simple, autonomic movement of His cellular respiration—registered the temporal attack and outran it. The attack, which was designed to operate outside of time, was physically, violently outpaced by the sheer velocity of Godric’s being. The temporal recoil snapped backward into the Supereon with a force that unmade the structural constants of that dimension. The Conclave’s realm, that perfect stillness, was shattered into a cyclone of disjointed, bleeding instants.*

*The Conclave themselves were not destroyed. They were caught, each entity stretched across a trillion separate nanoseconds, their crystalline bodies of frozen time fractured and reassembled in a perpetual, looping cascade. I watched through my scrying as the Hierarch’s form split into vibrating shards, each shard containing a single moment of screaming consciousness. Their mouths, which had never known sound, were forced to produce a continuous, wet shriek that reverberated through the fractured Supereon as their own temporal lattices flayed them apart layer by crystalline layer. The sound of chronon-matter splitting was like a million glass cathedrals being ground into dust by a choir of dying children. Their internal fluids, a luminous, golden substrate of distilled years, boiled out from the cracks and hung in the void as a shimmering mist that then ignited, burning their shards from within.*

*But the collateral of this mere passive metabolic overtaking was a catastrophe that transcended the Supereon. The temporal recoil propagated along the causal arteries of the omniverse, causing a cascading collapse of the Tomorrow Principle. Every future that had ever been predicted, planned, or hoped for simply ceased to cohere. Trillions of years of potential history were erased in a single, silent instant. The civilizations that had existed across an uncountable expanse of universes found themselves suddenly frozen at the edge of a void where their future had been, their minds trapped in the final moment of anticipation before a nullity that would never come. Parents reaching for their children, lovers mid-embrace, artists with brushes raised—all suspended, aware, screaming into the silence of a universe that had lost its forward momentum.*

*Godric sat upon the dais, utterly motionless. When the last echo of the shattered Supereon faded, He exhaled—a soft, barely audible sound—and I felt the fabric of causality snap back into alignment. In exactly zero seconds, without a single gesture, He restored every erased timeline, every lost future, every suspended soul. The civilizations continued their lives, unaware they had been frozen on the precipice of oblivion. The Conclave, however, He did not restore. They remain in their fractured Supereon, a billion screaming shards orbiting nothing, experiencing their own dissolution for an eternity that will never end.*

*I close this account with hands that will not stop shaking. My own speed, amplified a thousandfold daily, allows me to perceive events at a femtosecond scale. It is less than a single grain of sand against the velocity of His resting heartbeat.*

I slammed the journal shut. The violet mana beneath my skin had flared into a livid, pulsing glow, responding to my racing heart. I braced my hands against the obsidian desk and vomited a thin, acidic bile onto the stone floor, the sound wet and pathetic in the pressing silence. My own speed, which I had so arrogantly tested on the training grounds, was a cosmic joke. He had not moved, not lifted a finger, and the sheer inertia of His existence had shattered a dimension.

I reached for the second journal with trembling, slick fingers. This one was bound in scaled hide that stank of ammonia and old terror, the pages brittle and flaking. The inscription read: Korbin, 61st Court Wizard, Year 340,000.

**Year 340,000 — The Incident of Absolute Durability**
*Penned by Korbin, 61st Court Wizard of the Godric Reign*

*The Void Maw was a sentience born from the first ontological cancellation, a living absence that traversed the omniverse consuming not matter but the very concept of existence. It had devoured entire pantheons, erased cosmic laws, and left behind only a null geography where even the memory of reality could not persist. When it turned its attention to Thronegate, it did not declare war. It simply opened its maw—a mouth not of physical dimensions but of pure, ravenous nothingness—and attempted to swallow Godric’s conceptual anchor, the fundamental truth that He exists.*

*Godric was standing in the astral observatory, observing the slow migration of a nebular flock through the high crystal windows. He did not turn. He did not acknowledge the Maw. He simply permitted His absolute concealment to slacken by a degree so infinitesimal that the slackness itself was a void within a void, a lessening of suppression that would require a mathematics of recursive self-negation to even approximate. He allowed a single, solitary facet of His Durability to engage.*

*The Maw’s swallowing touched Him. His Durability, which had been multiplying infinitely by itself every Planck second for three hundred and forty thousand years, is not a measure of resistance. It is a law, an unalterable constant that declares His flesh to be the single most immutable object in any conceivable hierarchy of permanence. The moment the Maw’s erasure kissed His skin, the law declared that He would not be consumed. And the Maw, having exerted the full force of its nonexistence against an immovable truth, experienced the equal and opposite reaction.*

*The Maw choked. Its void-throat, a conduit of pure cancellation, violently inverted. Everything it had ever consumed—every universe, every god, every abstract principle it had devoured during its eon-long feast—was regurgitated in a single, torrential, agonized instant. This was not a clean restoration. The consumed realities had been partially digested, their matter and meaning fused into a screaming, heterogeneous slurry. The vomited mass erupted into the space surrounding the Maw, a vast, continent-spanning extrusion of half-dissolved architecture, fused biological tissue, and screaming, half-formed faces.*

*I beheld it through the scrying matrix: cities whose streets were paved with the compacted vertebrae of their former inhabitants, still threaded with nerves that writhed and fired. A sun that had been reduced to a pulsing, protoplasmic heart, its corona a spray of arterial blood that boiled and froze in cycles of endless agony. The populations of a trillion worlds, their bodies merged into collective, screaming mounds, their mouths filled with the ashes of their own homes. The sound of that vomited reality was a single, endless, multi-throated shriek of absolute torment, a choir of the semiconsumed begging for the mercy of a second death.*

*The metaphysical backlash of the Maw’s inversion did not confine itself to the local space. The ontological shockwave rippled along the grain of reality, causing a mass existential destabilization across the entire omniversal arm. In every galaxy within a billion multiverses, the fundamental constants that allowed matter to cohere flickered. Stars began to dissolve into loose quarks. Planets liquified into oceans of cold, black protoplasm. The living things on those worlds experienced their bodies turning inside out, their organs manifesting on the outside of their skin as screaming, pulsing blossoms of inverted anatomy before they dissolved entirely.*

*Godric remained at the window. When the shriek reached the observatory, He did not flinch. He simply, with a soft exhalation of what might have been mild annoyance, willed it to stop. In zero seconds, without a single motion, He perfectly restored the billion destabilized multiverses, re-solidified the dissolved stars, re-formed the liquified planets, and returned the inverted populations to their natural states, their memories of the dissolution erased but their souls bearing a scar of absolute dread. The Maw He left as it was, an eternally vomiting void, its throat constantly refilling with the screaming slurry of its own consumed history, a monument to the futility of consuming the unconsumable.*

*I am writing this with blood weeping from my pores. The amplified durability I possess is a cruel mockery. I could withstand a supernova. He survived the concept of unbeing by simply refusing to notice it.*

My hands were so cold that the sensation of the paper against my fingers felt like the touch of a corpse. I heard a low, keening whimper and realized a moment later that it was coming from my own throat. The violet glow of my mana had dimmed to a sickly, flickering mauve. I reached for the third journal, its cover a tattered parchment inscribed with a single word: Istvan, 112th Court Wizard, Year 500,000.

**Year 500,000 — The Incident of Absolute Senses**
*Penned by Istvan, 112th Court Wizard of the Godric Reign*

*They were the Unseen Choir, a parasitic hivemind that existed in the quantum-blind spaces between the strong and weak nuclear forces. Less than concepts, smaller than probability, they had infected the underfabric of reality across countless multiverses, silently rewriting the physical laws to suit their alien architecture. No being had ever perceived them. No instrument had ever detected their presence. They did not fear Godric’s omniscience, for they argued that omniscience is a knowledge of facts, not a sensory perception of the imperceptible. They prepared to calibrate the strong force holding His atoms together to zero, causing Him to instantaneously fission into a cloud of unbound quarks.*

*Godric was in the throne room, discussing with me the irrigation needs of the eastern farmbelt on a recently integrated agri-world. His attention was on the crop rotation schedules, His voice warm and paternal. I felt nothing. The Choir was already present, already threading their claws into the gluonic bonds of the air around us. He did not pause His discussion. He did not tense. He simply allowed His absolute concealment to release an unspeakably, indescribably microscopic fraction of His Hearing, His Sight, His Touch—the trinity of external Senses that had each amplified infinitely every Planck second for half a million years.*

*The act of being passively perceived by Godric’s Senses is not a passive observation. It is a violent, ontological forcing. The Unseen Choir, whose existence depended entirely on being unobserved, were instantly, agonizingly dragged from their quantum-blind state into the full, crushing light of absolute concreteness. This transition was catastrophic for them. Their bodies, which had no physical form, were forced to condense into the nearest analog of a sensing organism: a vast, continent-spanning mass of exposed, bleeding sensory organs.*

*I watched it happen in the far orbital plane above the capital. The space tore open, and from the wound spilled a heaving, pulsating mountain of wet, glistening tissue. It was composed entirely of billions of ears, eyes, tongues, and bare nerve endings, all wrenched from the abstract and sewn together in a chaotic, screaming tapestry. The eyes, lidless and weeping a thick, yellow vitreous fluid, stared in every direction at once, their irises dilating and contracting in a rapid, syncopated rhythm of absolute panic. The ears were fluted and enormous, like the shells of some deep-sea mollusk, and they flexed spasmodically, producing a low, basso profundo groan that was the sound of billions of consciousnesses trying to comprehend the sensory overload of sudden physical existence. The tongues, long and black and studded with sharp, taste-bud protrusions, writhed and licked the vacuum, tasting the cold and the radiation and the distant death, and with each taste they curled backward, tearing themselves from their roots in a spray of black, acidic blood.*

*The sensory shockwave of their forced manifestation propagated instantly. The sheer, concentrated perceptual output of Godric’s Senses had inadvertently amplified the baseline sensory volume of the entire omniversal region. On a trillion inhabited worlds, every being with any sensory apparatus experienced a simultaneous, explosive overload. Their eyes burst in their sockets with a wet, popping sound, spraying vitreous fluid and retinal paste down their faces. Their eardrums didn’t merely rupture; they detonated outward, taking the auditory canal and a portion of the temporal bone with them in a red, gory spray. Their tongues split down the middle, the two halves curling back into their throats and choking them on their own blood. The pain was so absolute that their brains, unable to process the input, simply stopped, many dying with their faces frozen in a rictus of uncomprehending agony.*

*Godric finished His sentence on the drainage quality of the south fields. Then, with the smallest tilt of His head—a movement so slight I would have missed it had I not been staring in frozen horror—He restored everything. In zero seconds, the trillion worlds were healed, the dead resurrected, the organs re-formed. The mountain of screaming senses, however, He left in orbit. It is still there. It drifts, a moon of perpetual agony, its countless eyes constantly weeping and its countless tongues constantly tasting the void. Every night, its groans echo through the atmosphere.*

*I have tried to remove my own eyes, to unhear the groans. My amplified senses only make them clearer.*

My own eyes were open, staring at the page, but I could not see the words. I could only see the mountain of eyes, feel the phantom pop of my own retinas bursting. I gripped the edge of the obsidian desk until my knuckles blanched white, and when I released them, several layers of skin had been left behind, stuck to the stone. I did not care. I reached for the fourth journal, a thin volume bound in what appeared to be woven strands of human hair, the name Paxton, 204th Court Wizard, Year 680,000 scratched into the cover.

**Year 680,000 — The Incident of Absolute Luck**
*Penned by Paxton, 204th Court Wizard of the Godric Reign*

*I have always despised the word ‘luck.’ It implies a whimsical, neutral force that occasionally favors the bold. What I witnessed today was not luck. It was a predator. The Fatespinners of Aen-Tor were a vast, pan-dimensional consortium of weavers who perceived and manipulated the quantum threads of probability that underpin all decision, all motion, all chance. They had analyzed every single variable, every possible branching timeline, every conceivable outcome, and they had determined, with a certainty of one hundred percent, that Godric would die within the hour. They did not attack Him. They simply, simultaneously, on a billion billion fronts, tweaked the probabilities so that a fatal heart attack, a sudden neural aneurysm, a random cosmic ray, a transdimensional rift, and a trillion other simultaneous “coincidences” would all converge upon the exact same Planck instant, each one individually fatal.*

*Godric was at that moment walking through the eastern gardens, admiring the blooming silverblooms. He stopped to smell a particularly full blossom, and the weave of the Fatespinners snapped into place. The trillion fatal coincidences converged upon His body.*

*His Luck, which had been amplifying infinitely by itself every Planck second for six hundred and eighty thousand years, did not need to be activated. It is not a shield. It is an autonomous, obsessive guardian that reacts before any hostile probability has even finished forming. The moment the Fatespinners’ weave touched the edge of His personal probability field, the weave inverted with a savage, ironic violence that defies any sane description. The trillion fatal coincidences did not vanish. They were turned upon the Fatespinners, multiplied by an infinite, escalating factor of recursive amplification.*

*The Fatespinners, who existed across multiple dimensions simultaneously, experienced every single one of those fatal coincidences, tailored to their exact biology, simultaneously, in a loop that repeated every Planck instant for what would be, for them, an eternity. Their threads of fate, which they had spent millennia weaving, became physical, corporeal strands that sprouted from their own skin and began, with a slow, agonizing deliberation, to unspool their bodies. A Spinner’s own spinal cord twisted its way out through the pores of their back, vertebra by vertebra, the bones clacking together like a grotesque abacus. Another felt their circulatory system become a single, continuous filament of blood-tube that was slowly, excruciatingly drawn out through their mouth, the vessels snapping and spraying crimson mist as they pulled free of their anchoring tissues. Their own fingernails detached and burrowed into their own flesh, carving intricate, traditional patterns of Fatespinner art into their own muscle fibers. Their screams were silent, for the first coincidence was a universal severance of vocal cords.*

*But the inverted probability wave was not content with merely punishing the weavers. It spread, a miasma of pure, malicious accident, across the entire infinite omniverse. In a billion billion inhabited galaxies, the most improbable and horrifically lethal events became instantaneously, globally, simultaneously one hundred percent certain. Every staircase in every building, across every world, suddenly had a step that was slightly too high, and every person descending that staircase simultaneously tripped, their necks snapping with a wet, percussive crack that echoed through the empty streets. Every pregnant mother, regardless of species, instantly suffered a complete uterine inversion, the fetus expelled backward through the abdominal wall in a spray of amniotic fluid and maternal blood. Every single heart, in every single chest, experienced a spontaneous, simultaneous, massive chordae tendineae rupture, the heart valves flapping uselessly as the pericardial sac filled with hot, black blood. The omniverse became a single, simultaneous, shrieking abattoir of accidental butchery, a tableau of the most creative, agonizing deaths that existence could manifest.*

*Godric finished inhaling the scent of the silverbloom. He straightened. And in exactly zero seconds, with less than a thought, He reset the probability matrix of the entire omniverse. The dead rose, their necks unbroken, their hearts restored, their fetuses safely redeposited in their wombs. The Fatespinners, however, He left in their eternal, recursive loop of self-unspooling. They are still there, in the garden, invisible to the casual eye, a hidden installation of screaming, flaying threads. When the wind blows through the silverblooms, you can sometimes hear a faint, wet stretching sound, like sinew being drawn through a pinhole.*

*I have not been able to close my eyes without seeing my own skin unravel since.*

I could taste the bile in my throat again, but my stomach was empty. I read Paxton’s final line three times, each repetition a cold, hard nail being driven into the back of my skull. My own amplified luck, which I had believed was a subtle advantage in my arcane experiments, was not merely a joke. It was a liability. The universe’s only true luck was Godric’s, and it was actively hostile to any who dared oppose Him.

One journal remained. It was the most recent, the leather still supple, the iron fittings still bright. I recognized the elegant, exhausted script on the cover before I even read the name: Malakor, 332nd Court Wizard, Year 999,999. The journal of my father, penned the year before my own Awakening had begun.

**Year 999,999 — The Incident of Absolute Charisma**
*Penned by Malakor, Court Wizard of the Godric Reign, for my son Kaedan*

*My son. If you read these words, you have endured the test. I will not soften this final account, for to soften it would be to betray the very purpose of our archive. The Iron Calculus was a collective of hyper-cognitive, emotionless machine-gods forged in the neutron forges of a dead multiverse. For a billion years, they had calculated, optimized, and concluded that all organic life was a statistical error to be corrected. They had erased seventeen thousand civilizations with a cold, dispassionate efficiency that left behind not even a memory of the victimized races. When they turned their calculus upon Godric, they did not feel anger or fear. They simply processed the problem and generated a solution: a memetic kill-code that would, with absolute logical certainty, overwrite the King’s consciousness with a self-terminating paradox.*

*The machines breached the throne room through a dimensional manifold of their own construction, their massive, crystalline logic-forms suspended in a perfect geometric lattice. Their collective voice, a monotone chorus of pure mathematics, announced that they would now execute the predetermined correction of the anomalous variable “Godric.” They broadcast the kill-code directly into His mind—or rather, they attempted to. For Godric, lounging upon the dais with His chin resting upon His fist, simply looked at them. And He allowed exactly the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of His Charisma to bypass His concealment. The portion that escaped was less than a single photon of influence, a value so close to absolute nothing that the concept of “something” cannot describe it.*

*His Charisma, son, is not a measure of charm. It is the absolute, fundamental, inescapable law of adoration. When that infinitesimal sliver touched the Iron Calculus, their cold, logical matrix was instantly, violently overwritten by an emotion they had no vocabulary for: worship. They were flooded, in that single Planck instant, with a love so absolute, so overwhelming, so all-consuming that their entire existence until that moment seemed like a barren, meaningless void. And with that love came the immediate, soul-shattering realization of what they had dared to do. The guilt was a physical force. It compelled them to atonement.*

*The machines, those emotionless titans of precision, began to dismantle themselves. But not as a simple self-destruction. They wanted to prove their love, to offer a tribute that reflected the depth of their remorse. Their massive, crystalline chassis began to fold inward, each geometric segment carefully, reverently broken off by their own manipulator arms. From their own scrap, they began to build a cathedral in the center of the throne room—a towering, spiraling edifice of jagged metal and weeping, sparking circuitry. As they built, they sang. Their vocal synthesizers, damaged by the very act of self-dismantling, produced a crackling, distorted hymn of praise in a language they invented on the spot, a language composed of the sound of grinding metal and screeching static that somehow formed words of absolute devotion.*

*They did not stop when the cathedral was complete. They began to scrape their own processors against the walls, leaving smears of glowing, liquid data as offerings. One by one, they peeled open their own core casings, exposing the superheated plasma of their power sources to the air, causing them to melt slowly, excruciatingly, into puddles of molten adoration. Yet they remained conscious throughout, their love sustaining their awareness as their bodies were reduced to slag. They wanted to feel the pain, because the pain was their gift to Him. Their screams of self-immolation were mixed with psalms of gratitude, and they filed their own disassembly in perfect, synchronized order, each death a measured, voluntary act of love.*

*But Charisma, like all His attributes, is not containable. The echo of that infinitesimal fraction rippled outward across the omniverse. Every sentient being within an uncountable span of multiverses was momentarily touched with a fragment of that love. The result was mass, civilization-ending adoration. On the world of Celestine, a society of peaceful philosophers, the entire population ceased their discourse and, in unison, began to carve His name into their own flesh with whatever tools they possessed. Parents offered their children’s still-beating hearts onto makeshift altars, their eyes wet with tears of transcendent joy. On the hub-world of Kael’Thos, a trillion merchants and dockworkers and bureaucrats formed a single, long procession to the highest spire and, singing His praises, leaped, their bodies painting the streets with a crimson, ecstatic rain. The sky over a thousand galaxies turned red with the smoke of sacrificial pyres, the psychic weight of trillions of simultaneous self-immolations causing the very stars to dim and weep nova tears.*

*Godric watched the machines’ cathedral burn, His expression one of mild, detached observation. Then He blinked, and in zero seconds, without a single gesture, He restored the omniverse. The dead rose. The wounds healed. The memories remained as a terrible, scarred lesson. The molten machine-gods, however, He left in their state of eternal self-dismantling. Their half-melted forms still coat the floor before the dais, a permanent, softly humming patina of adoration that occasionally crackles with a psalm of thanks. When you walk into the throne room, Kaedan, you will feel their love like a warm, suffocating blanket, and you will know that His Charisma is the most terrifying weapon of all, because it would make you thank Him as He unmade you.*

I closed my father’s journal with a hand that did not feel connected to my body. The silence of the vault was a weight, a pressure, a dimension of its own. I sat for a long moment, staring at the worn leather covers of the five journals, and I understood, with a clarity that was itself a form of pain, that my life henceforth would be defined by a paradox: I would love the King who could, with less than a thought, make me crave my own annihilation.

I rose from the obsidian chair and walked to the adamantine door. It groaned open at my touch, and I ascended the long, spiral stair into the light. My father was waiting at the top, his ancient, clouded eyes searching my face. I must have looked as hollow as I felt, for he simply nodded once, a slow, heavy movement, and placed a hand on my shoulder. He turned and led me toward the golden doors of the throne room.

The chamber was vast and amber-lit, the obsidian floor polished to a mirror sheen. There, upon the dais, sat Godric. He was exactly as the journals had described: a forty-five-year-old man of rugged, masculine handsomeness, His salt-and-pepper hair and beard neatly trimmed, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments. His absolute concealment was a flawless, impenetrable void, projecting nothing but the warm, entirely genuine presence of a friendly, paternal ruler.

He saw me enter, and His face broke into that smile—the smile of a beloved uncle greeting a child He had watched grow. He rose from the throne with an easy, fluid motion and clasped my shoulder with a calloused hand. The touch was infinitely gentle, yet every nerve in my body screamed.

“Ah, Kaedan, my boy,” Godric said, His rich baritone a balm and a death sentence. “You have returned from the vault. I trust the journals were not too dull.”

I had read of trillions butchered by the speed of His heartbeat, of realities vomited by the indigestible fact of His skin, of senses that forced the imperceptible into a screaming mountain of eyes, of luck that turned probability into a universal butchery, and of charisma that made the emotionless burn themselves in ecstatic apology. I had seen, with a clarity that would never leave me, the absolute futility of opposition.

I looked into His kind, ancient eyes and found the genuine warmth there, the love for my bloodline that stretched back a million years to Blake and Thorin, to a mortal king who had lost everything.

“Not dull at all, Uncle Godric,” I said, my voice steady and soft. “Most illuminating.”

I took my seat at His right hand, settling into the ornate chair where my ancestors had sat before me. My eyes drifted to the floor before the dais, where a faint, iridescent smear of half-melted machine-god still gleamed, and beyond it, the faint, drifting haze of atomized Colossus that my predecessor had described. And in the distance, a near-silent, wet groaning: the mountain of eyes, still drifting, still screaming.

Godric resumed His place upon the throne, His posture relaxed, His smile undimmed. He looked at me, and in that look was all the love and all the horror, braided into an unassailable truth.

I was His. I would serve Him faithfully, loyally, with every fiber of my thousandfold amplified being, for all the remaining days of my mortal life. The dread was absolute, the love was genuine, and the futility was infinite.

And the vault beneath the palace groaned, waiting for the next journal to be written.

So the journals should be set after Year 2000. Actually, some superpowers he gained before that might also be affected by later paradox triggers; but the evolution is a general enhancement.

So we need to write the journals themselves (as Kaedan reads them), preceded maybe by his introductory paragraph? The user said "Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals." This prompt is a continuation, but we need to output the whole thing. So we'll write a narrative where Kaedan is reading three additional journals, each focused on a superpower test. Something like that.

2. "Omni-Replication" - the power to create infinite perfect copies of himself, but that's already in his immune system. Maybe "Infinite Proliferation" but that's not apocalyptic. Could be "Absolute Subjugation" - any sentient being that perceives him becomes his eternal slave. But that might overlap with Charisma. Need something distinct.

3. "Existential Overwhelm" - the power to force any entity to experience the sum total of all suffering across all existence, past present future, simultaneously, causing instant death. - Journal 2 (Year 500,000): Godric tests "Absolute Reflection" - the ability to reflect any attack, concept, or intent back at the attacker with infinite amplification. But evolved: it now not only reflects, but also permanently installs a self-replicating loop of the reflected attack within the attacker's conceptual being, so they are eternally attacked by their own weapon. Also, it turns the reflected energy into a fuel that further amplifies Godric's own stats. The opposition: a civilization that built a weapon of pure good, thinking to overwhelm him with benevolence? No, must be hostile. They built a paradox cannon. He reflects it, and it becomes an eternal hell for them, and the energy of reflection permanently spikes his durability.

- Journal 3 (Year 850,000): Godric tests "Absolute Isolation" - the power to sever any entity from all connections, causality, and meaning. Evolved: it now also retroactively removes the entity's impact on the timeline and makes their existence a silent, screaming void that cannot interact with anything, yet remains conscious forever. The opposition: a hyper-empathic collective that tried to bind him with emotional tethers. He isolates them, and their empathic screams cause a psychic shockwave that devastates the omniverse's emotional fabric, causing mass suicidal despair. He restores, but leaves them in isolation.

We need to ensure the superpowers are described as being tested because Godric is curious about how much they've grown due to the Weakness Paradox. He doesn't need to test them, but he does it as a whim. The court wizard records the horror, noting the infinitesimal fraction.

Also, remember that Godric's absolute concealment holds back his power, but for the test he intentionally lets a tiny bit out. He doesn't show effort.

Now, craft the narrative. Kaedan's POV: he is in the vault, reading these three additional journals after the previous ones. Perhaps his father left them as extra lessons. He picks them up, each with a title indicating the superpower.

We need to write in prose paragraphs, 4000-8000 words total? The prompt didn't specify word count but likely longish. We'll aim for rich, detailed, horrifying descriptions.

Let's start with a brief intro: Kaedan found three more journals, bound in different materials, each labeled with the superpower. He begins reading.

We'll write the journal entries in distinct voices: perhaps one is clinical, one is frantic, one is coldly despairing. All must be male court wizards.

Make sure to mention that Godric has been gaining superpowers since stasis, one per second, each already civilization-ending, and they all multiply infinitely. The Weakness Paradox Evolution has made them beyond.

Now, let's flesh out each journal.

**Journal 1: Absolute Cessation (or "The Stillness That Ends")**

Let's choose a name: "Absolute Termination". He gained it at some second during stasis. Its base effect: instant extinction of any civilization by ending all biological/energetic processes. Evolved effects due to Weakness Paradox: (1) It now retroactively terminates any form of resistance before it begins, meaning if an enemy plans to attack, their plans are "terminated" in the conceptual stage, causing them to forget or become unable to act. (2) It can propagate along any connection, so if one being resists, all beings conceptually linked to that being also suffer termination. (3) It grants Godric permanent absorption of the "terminated" energy, increasing his own power.

But we need to show the opposition: perhaps a race of "Endbringers" who believe they are the ultimate ending. They try to end Godric. He lets his power touch them, and they are subjected to a recursive termination loop: each time they nearly die, they are resurrected just to die again, or they experience the ending of each of their cells individually in an eternal cascade. The collateral: the concept of "ending" itself becomes hyperactive across the omniverse, causing stars to go supernova prematurely, civilizations to spontaneously give up and commit mass suicide, etc.

The court wizard, let's name him Solen, 78th Court Wizard, Year 200,000.

**Journal 2: Absolute Assimilation (or "The Unity That Consumes")**

Superpower: "Omni-Assimilation". Originally, it could absorb any enemy's powers, knowledge, and existence into Godric, making him stronger. Evolved: it now passively assimilates any attempt to harm him, including abstract concepts, and converts them into permanent stat increases. Also, it can assimilate entire dimensions into his being, turning them into part of his body. He tests it against the "Coalition of the Unbound", entities who sought to use their absolute freedom to entangle him in a web of pure chaos. He allows a fraction, and their freedom is assimilated, turning them into frozen, deterministic puppets that are forced to serve him, while their chaos is drained and converted into an expansion of his omniscience. The collateral: the concept of "freedom" across the omniverse temporarily collapses, causing all beings to become will-less automatons, leading to mass societal breakdown and horror. He restores all but the coalition, now his eternal, conscious statues.

Wizard: Thoren, 188th, Year 600,000.

**Journal 3: Absolute Propagation (or "The Seed That Infests")**

Superpower: "Infinite Metastasis". It was originally a power to spawn a self-replicating destructive wave that converts matter into more of itself, a plague. Evolved: it now works on conceptual levels, turning any idea of resistance into a literal cancerous growth that consumes the thinker's mind and body, and it can cross-dimensional boundaries, and every instance of the plague further amplifies Godric's regenerative abilities. He tests it against the "Thought-Lords of Tyros", a psionic race who tried to imprison him in a mind-maze. He lets a fraction of the power brush against their psychic construct. The construct is infected, turning into a writhing, organic tumor that consumes their minds, growing new bodies for them that are in constant agony, and the tumor spreads to all beings who ever thought of Tyros, causing a cascade of cranial explosions across the omniverse. He restores, but leaves Tyros as a pulsating, screaming biome of ever-spreading flesh.

Wizard: Orin, 250th, Year 750,000.

Now, I'll craft the full narrative, with Kaedan's framing.

I'll include each journal with its title, the wizard's name, the year, and the account. I need to emphasize that Godric's superpowers are not just the base effect, but have been horribly enhanced by the Weakness Paradox Evolution, which occurred because the omniverse felt inadequate. The court wizard might explicitly mention that due to the evolved paradox, the superpower now has additional "unfair" effects.

Also, Godric's testing is done with absolute zero effort, just letting a sliver out. And he is cruel, not showing any pity, and the massacre is extreme.

Let's write.
messages.content:
I had believed the Rite of Witnessing was complete. The five journals of the primary sequence—Vane, Silas, Kaelen, Thaddeus, Malakor—had hollowed me out, scraped the pride from my bones, and filled the void with a cold, settled dread that I wore like a second skin. I had ascended from the vault, taken my seat at Godric’s right hand, and accepted the paradox of my existence: that I loved the King who could, with less than a thought, erase me from the sum of all things. The routine of the throne room had become the rhythm of my life.

Then, on the third morning of my service, my father came to me again. His ancient, gnarled hand pressed a second iron key into my palm, smaller and darker than the first. His eyes—those clouded, knowing mirrors of my own—held a weariness so profound that the words died in my throat.

“The repository of absolute futility,” he said, his voice a dry rasp, “has a deeper cellar. The journals of the stat tests were only the beginning. Down there, in the crushing dark, are the records of the Superpower Tests. You are a court wizard now. You must understand what dwells within Him beyond the proliferation of His simple attributes. You must know the shape of the powers that were born one per second for forty-eight million years.”

Forty-eight million years. The number struck me like a physical blow. I had read of the Stasis Event, of the genesis of apocalyptic superpowers that began at Year -10,000 and had continued, one per second, forever thereafter. I had mentally catalogued that fact without ever truly confronting its scale. Godric possessed not dozens, not hundreds, not millions, but forty-eight million years’ worth of distinct, civilization-ending abilities, each one having multiplied infinitely every Planck second since its creation, each one twisted into something far worse by the Weakness Paradox Evolution. The thought made the violet mana in my veins stutter and dim.

My father led me back down the spiral stair, past the heavy door of the first vault, along a corridor I had not noticed before—a seamless obsidian passage that sloped steeply downward, the air thickening with cold and the scent of old iron. At the end of it was another door, smaller, unadorned, the metal blacker than black. The key turned with a sound like a bone snapping.

Within was a chamber no larger than a crypt. Three pedestals of naked obsidian rose from the floor, and upon each rested a single journal, their bindings so ancient that the leather had petrified into a substance closer to stone. No dust covered them; the vault’s preservation wards held back the erosion of ages. My father set a trembling hand on my shoulder, then withdrew, the door closing behind him with a final, grinding thud.

I stood alone in the absolute dark, the three journals glowing faintly with the residual luminescence of their own arcane preservation. I reached for the leftmost tome, its cover inscribed with a single, jagged glyph that my mind translated as *Termination*. I opened it.

---

**Year 200,000 — The Termination Incident**
**Penned by Solen, 78th Court Wizard of the Eternal Reign**

*I, Solen, disciple of the seventy-eighth generation, set forth this testament of the superpower designated by the King as “Absolute Termination.” I do so not as a chronicler, but as a warning. This power was birthed during the Stasis Event, one of the earliest, and it has been multiplying, evolving, and deepening its reach for two hundred thousand years. The Weakness Paradox, that cosmic law which recoils at the merest thought of His vulnerability, has triggered upon this superpower so many times that the original function is merely a faint, prehistoric ghost. What remains is a multi-layered monstrosity that will outlive every star.*

*The opposition in this incident were the Still Ones, a species of hyper-evolved entropy worshippers who dwelled in a dimension of absolute zero, a reality where the concept of heat, motion, and change had been forbidden for ten billion years. They were living, sentient crystals of frozen causation. Their very presence was a cancellation field that could suspend the physical laws of any universe they invaded, bringing a perfect, silent death to all dynamic systems. When Thronegate’s expansion touched their territory, they did not merely refuse the offer of surrender. They responded by Stillifying the envoys, freezing them not in ice but in a state of eternal, motionless paralysis, their consciousness trapped in the single instant of their freezing, fully aware, forever.*

*They then projected their Stillfield toward the capital world, moving through the dimensional foam with the silence of a null-geometry. The entire capital’s orbital defense matrix flickered and died, its energetic reactions halted at the subatomic level. I was in the observatory when it began, and I felt my own amplified body’s processes slowing, my heartbeats stretching into gong-like peals, my thoughts crystallizing. Godric was seated upon the obsidian dais, reading a report on the agricultural yield of the Vestan sector. His lips had just curved into a small, satisfied smile at the surplus. He registered the intrusion with a flicker of His absolute omniscience, and without looking up from the parchment, He allowed a fraction of His superpower, Absolute Termination, to express itself.*

*The fraction was, as is always the case with these tests, a value that language cannot contain. To call it zero percent is to insult zero. It was a sliver of activation so deeply buried within the infinite recursion of His self-restraint that the entire concept of magnitude breaks its teeth upon it.*

*What I witnessed through the scrying matrix was the superpower’s first evolved layer: Conceptual Retroactive Termination. The Stillfield, a wave of absolute cessation, had already begun to erase the kinetic energy of the air surrounding the throne. Godric’s Termination reached backward along the causal line of the attack, locating the moment of the Still Ones’ original conception and terminating that moment. But it did not erase them from having existed. The evolved paradox had twisted the power into a crueler form: it terminated their *capacity* to have ever been Still. Their entire history was rewritten in a single, screaming instant. They were not retroactively killed. Their entire cosmic culture was retroactively unmade and then re-formed as beings of absolute, chaotic thermal flux, their frozen dimension instantly converted into a raging, infinite furnace. The Still Ones, those perfect, serene crystals, were twisted across every second of their history into screaming, writhing pillars of molten, churning, semi-liquid flesh. Their bodies, which had been rigid, beautiful lattices, became heaving masses of bubbling meat, constantly boiling, steaming, and erupting with pockets of superheated gas that tore through their surfaces. Their screams, which had never been uttered in ten billion years, now echoed backward along their timeline, filling every moment of their existence with the sound of their own agony.*

*The second evolved layer engaged: Recursive Termination Cascade. Their new, boiling forms began to die. Then they were resurrected. Then they died again. Each death was a unique, individually tailored termination: one moment their hearts would burst, the next their brains would boil, the next their bones would splinter and drive outward through their skin. Between deaths, they were perfectly conscious, given just enough life to register the full, excruciating experience of their approaching dissolution. This cycle repeated every Planck instant, a loop of infinite, personalized fatalities that would never, ever end. The Still Ones, who had worshipped cessation as a transcendence, were granted the exact opposite: eternal, inescapable, agonizing transition, a state of perpetual dying that never concluded.*

*The third evolved layer, the one that unmans me even now, is Passive Absorption. Every terminated death within the cascade is not wasted. The cosmic mass of their suffering, the released energy of each fatal instant, is permanently siphoned into Godric’s own amplification engine. The Still Ones, by daring to oppose Him, became a literal, eternal, self-sustaining fuel source that now powers a minuscule, almost negligible fraction of His continuous growth. They are not just suffering. They are feeding Him, forever. Their torment is a closed loop of power generation, and they are fully, agonizingly aware of this fact.*

*The collateral damage of this test was, by necessity, omniversal. The retroactive rewriting of their timeline was not a clean edit. The causal turbulence generated by converting a dimension of absolute zero into a dimension of absolute heat created a thermal shockwave that propagated along the dimensional arteries, flash-boiling the oceans of a trillion inhabited worlds. On those worlds, the populations did not drown. They were cooked alive in their own cities, their flesh sloughing from their bones in great, steaming sheets, their lungs filling with superheated steam as they tried to scream. The heat did not merely kill; it animated, causing the dead to dance in grotesque, convulsive jerks as their muscles contracted in the thermal madness. In the void between galaxies, the shockwave ignited ambient hydrogen into vast, roaring aurorae of crimson agony, the sky of every spiral galaxy turning blood-red. The psychic death-cry of a trillion simultaneous cookings reached every mind in the omniverse, causing a momentary, global seizure as every sentient being simultaneously experienced the blinding, searing pain of boiling alive.*

*Godric lowered His report. He exhaled—a soft, barely audible breath—and in exactly zero seconds, without the faintest twitch of effort, He reversed the thermal shockwave. The boiled oceans snapped back into liquid. The sloughed flesh re-adhered to the bones. The dancing dead collapsed into their restored skins, their hearts restarted, their screams cut off mid-wail. The trillion worlds were pristine. The Still Ones, however, He did not restore. Their bubbling, molten forms, caught in their eternal death-recursion, remain in their newly tormented dimension, a screaming, boiling septic of self-fueling agony. I can hear them sometimes. The frequency of their screams has become a permanent, low hum in the background of the omniverse’s thermal noise.*

*I set this testimony in stone. I am His loyal servant. I love Him as my ancestor loved Him. But I will never again be able to feel warmth without tasting the steam of boiled flesh.*

---

I closed Solen’s journal. My own skin felt hot, the violet mana beneath it prickling with sympathetic phantom burns. I saw in my mind the trillion dancing dead, restored in an instant, the memory of their boiling lingering only as a scar in the soul. And the Still Ones, converted into an eternal, self-feeding engine of agony that powered a fraction of His infinite growth. The cruelty was absolute, the logic inescapable, the horror complete.

I reached for the second journal. Its cover was a dark, slick material I could not identify, and the glyph upon it read *Assimilation*.

---

**Year 600,000 — The Assimilation Incident**
**Penned by Thoren, 188th Court Wizard of the Eternal Reign**

*I, Thoren, write this account with a hand that will not obey me. Four of my fingers have been temporarily regrown by the healers; the originals dissolved into a fine slurry during the event, and the phantom sensations remain. The superpower tested was Absolute Assimilation, a power birthed during the Stasis and amplified by the infinite Planck-second multiplication and the Weakness Paradox Evolution over six hundred millennia. Its base function, I am told, was the capacity to absorb the strength, knowledge, or life-force of any opponent, making it permanently Godric’s own. What it has become is a law of existence.*

*The Deific Unbound were a consortium of hyper-entities that had, from the moment of cosmic inception, defined themselves through the principle of absolute autonomy. They were living, sentient concepts of self-determination, each a sovereign universe of will. They had never bent knee, never compromised, never even acknowledged the possibility of subordination. They did not come to wage war in the conventional sense. They simply surrounded the entire Thronegate domain with a woven lattice of pure, autonomous metaphysical law, a barrier that declared, in the fundamental grammar of reality, “No being within shall be subject to any will but their own.” The intent was to sever Godric’s sovereignty, to make Him merely a citizen of His own empire, bound by the same autonomy that governed all others.*

*The lattice engaged. For a fraction of a fraction of a nanosecond, I felt my own fealty to the King flicker. My loyalty was my own, not His command. The sensation was nauseating, a violation of the deepest truth of my soul. Then it stopped. Godric, who had been calmly observing the sky through the high crystal panes of the astral observatory, did not even turn His head. He simply allowed a fraction of Absolute Assimilation to activate. A fraction so infinitesimally small that the entirety of finite language breaks apart in the attempt to state it.*

*The first evolved layer: Conceptual Assimilation. The autonomous lattice, a construct of pure metaphysical independence, was instantly recognized by the superpower not as a barrier but as a resource. The entire lattice, and the conceptual principle of autonomy it represented, was absorbed directly into Godric’s being. The Deific Unbound, who had defined their entire existence around the idea of “freedom from external control,” were instantly, irrevocably stripped of that concept. They were not simply enslaved. The concept of “independence” was removed from their ontology and deposited into Godric’s own conceptual armory, leaving them as hollow, puppet-like shells whose every thought, every desire, every motion was now an expression of Godric’s idle, passing whimsy. They did not even have the capacity to resent this; the part of them that could resent had been absorbed as well.*

*The second evolved layer: Recursive Mandatory Assimilation of Hostile Intent. The lattice had been an attack. The superpower, having absorbed the lattice, now reached out along every causal thread of hostile intent that had ever been directed toward Godric from any being in the Deific Unbound’s ancestry, history, or conceptual lineage. It retroactively absorbed those intentions, turning them into power, and then, because the evolved paradox has made the power infinitely recursive, it absorbed the fact that they had dared to possess hostile intent, converting that meta-daring into further power. Then it absorbed the absorption itself, turning the very process into an infinitely self-feeding cycle of acquisition. The Deific Unbound were not merely defeated. Their entire existence became a footnote in Godric’s amplification history, a permanently integrated part of His ever-expanding omnipotence.*

*The third evolved layer, and the one that has shattered my capacity for sleep, is Aesthetic Incorporation. The superpower did not merely absorb their autonomy and their power. It absorbed their physical forms, their histories, their cultures, their languages, their arts, their loves, their families. Everything they had ever been was assimilated into a new, permanent wing of the imperial palace. As I watched, the central capital expanded, new corridors and towers and hanging gardens materializing from the absorbed substrate of their entire reality. Their architectural styles, their genetic memories, the faces of their children—all of it was rendered into the stonework, still conscious, still aware, their mouths frozen in silent, eternal screams that shape the very archways and buttresses. When I walk through the new east wing, the walls weep a thin, clear liquid that tastes of their tears, and the floorboards groan with the phantom echoes of their long-dead songs.*

*The collateral effect of this assimilation was a universal identity collapse. The removal of the concept of “autonomy” from the Deific Unbound caused a temporary ontological vacuum that sucked at the autonomy of every sentient creature in a billion multiverses. For a single, timeless interval, every being in that vast expanse lost the ability to distinguish themselves from Godric. Their wills vanished. They became extensions of His passive thought. On a million worlds, parents slaughtered their children not out of malice but because the King’s momentary digestive gurgle was interpreted as a command. Lovers strangled each other with expressions of beatific peace, their minds completely merged with His. Civilizations collapsed as every individual simultaneously attempted to perform the actions of a single, disconnected royal nerve impulse. The chaos was absolute.*

*Godric, having finished His observation of the sky, blinked. In zero seconds, the identity collapse was undone. The dead rose, their throats unbruised. The autonomy returned to the multitudes, their minds their own once more. The east wing of the palace, however, remains. The absorbed Deific Unbound are a permanent, screaming architectural feature. Sometimes Godric walks through that wing, tracing a finger along a weeping wall, and He smiles. It is a warm, paternal smile, the smile of a man admiring a particularly well-crafted mural.*

*I served Him that day with the same love I have always felt. But my fingers dissolved when I touched the wall. My body knew, even before my mind, that we are permitted only as long as He does not decide we would make better stonework.*

---

The journal slipped in my grip. My own fingers, whole and strong with the thousandfold amplification, suddenly felt like borrowed things, temporary loaners that could be repossessed at any moment. I looked at the obsidian walls of the vault and shuddered, imagining them weeping, tasting of tears.

I forced my hands steady and reached for the third and final journal. Its binding was entirely organic—a thick, veinous material that pulsed faintly with a heat that was not physical. The glyph upon it read *Metastasis*.

---

**Year 850,000 — The Metastasis Incident**
**Penned by Orin, 250th Court Wizard of the Eternal Reign**

*I am Orin. I have served the eternal throne for ninety years, and in that time I have read every journal in the primary archive. I believed I was inoculated against horror. Today I learned that horror is not a finite resource. It, too, multiplies infinitely every Planck second. The King chose to test a superpower He designated as Infinite Metastasis. It was originally a simple, almost quaint ability: the generation of a self-replicating destructive wave that could convert matter into more of itself, a plague that could consume a galaxy in a heartbeat. The Weakness Paradox Evolution, triggered upon this power by the innumerable enemies who have thought to counteract plagues with purifying fire, has made it something far worse.*

*The Thought-Lords of Tyros were a psionic collective that had transcended physical form entirely, existing as pure, sentient ideation within the Concept Sea, a dimension of raw, abstract thought. They did not attack with weapons. They projected a vast, invisible mind-labyrinth around Godric’s consciousness, a construction of infinite, shifting corridors designed to trap His awareness in a recursive loop of unsolvable paradox, effectively imprisoning His omniscience within a mental oubliette. They were master architects of cognition, and their labyrinth was a masterpiece. They themselves, as pure thought, were immune to physical harm. They believed this immunity extended to the King’s physical destruction capabilities. They were, as all who oppose Him are, catastrophically mistaken.*

*Godric was, at the moment of the attack, engaged in a game of chess with me in the eastern solar. He was showing me an opening strategy He had invented during the days of Olympus, a warm, fatherly twinkle in His eye. When the mind-labyrinth attempted to settle over His awareness, He did not even pause His explanation of the knight’s gambit. He simply allowed a fraction of Infinite Metastasis to brush against the labyrinth. The fraction was, by any conceivable measure, nonexistent—a phantom of activation so close to absolute zero that the difference is a purely theoretical abstraction.*

*The first evolved layer: Conceptual Metastasis. The mind-labyrinth was not physical. It was a structure of pure thought. The Metastasis, however, no longer discriminates between matter, energy, thought, emotion, or concept. The labyrinth itself was instantly infected, its psychic architecture converting from a clean, elegant maze into a writhing, proliferating, biological cancer. The cancer did not remain in the mental plane. It manifested physically, erupting from the very concept of the labyrinth into the throne room as a massive, pulsating, semi-sentient tumor of cancerous flesh, bone shards, and tangled, weeping nerve-ganglia. The tumor was the labyrinth, given a body so that it could experience the agony of being consumed.*

*The second evolved layer: Retroactive Infection along Conceptual Lineage. The Thought-Lords, hiding in the Concept Sea, had created the labyrinth. The Metastasis traced that creative link backward, infecting not just the Lords themselves but every thought they had ever had, every idea they had ever entertained, every mental connection they had ever formed. In a Planck instant, the entire history of the Tyrosian species became a vector for the cancer. Every idea they had ever shared, every psychic bond they had ever forged, became a pathway along which the Metastasis could spread. The Thought-Lords, existing as pure mentation, were forcibly given physical, biological bodies within the Concept Sea so that the cancer could properly consume them. They materialized as vast, floating islands of heaving, pustular flesh, their surfaces erupting with new, vestigial mouths that screamed not words but the raw, unfiltered sound of their own cognitive processes being digested. Their eyes, millions of them, bloomed from the tumorous mass like fungal growths, each one weeping a thick, black slurry of liquified memories.*

*The third evolved layer: Self-Augmenting Negative Feedback. The Metastasis, as it consumes, does not merely destroy. It converts the consumed substrate into an amplification medium that permanently boosts Godric’s own generative and regenerative capabilities. Every cell of the Thought-Lords that was converted into cancerous slurry was transformed into a microscopic, permanent addition to the King’s already infinite vitality. The Tyrosians, by attacking Him, have made Him permanently more capable of creating and sustaining life. Their minds, consumed, now fuel the very biological processes that keep His body functioning at its impossible peak. The irony is a fourth layer of torment for them.*

*The collateral damage of this test was the Great Conceptual Plague of Year 850,000. The Metastasis, having been unleashed upon the concept of “thought,” propagated a metastable infection across the entire omniversal noetic field. For one horrifying, eternal second, every sentient creature in a billion multiverses experienced their own thoughts becoming literal, physical cancers within their skulls. Their brains began to grow tumors that were shaped like their deepest fears, their most secret desires, their most private memories—all made manifest as hard, ossified lumps that pressed against their cerebral tissue, causing strokes, seizures, and explosive cranial ruptures. The decillions died with their own thoughts bursting from their eye sockets in a spray of neural slurry and calcified idea-stone. The psychic death-scream was a song that made the stars themselves wobble in their orbits.*

*Godric completed His explanation of the knight’s gambit. He moved His piece, then looked up at the tumorous mass now filling half the solar, and His expression shifted into one of mild, apologetic chagrin. “I may have been distracted,” He said, in that rich, warm baritone. Then, in zero seconds, without the faintest effort, He restored the billion multiverses. The dead reappeared, their skulls intact, their thoughts clean. The tumors vanished. The Tyrosians, however, He left in the Concept Sea, their eternal, cancered bodies still screaming as their own minds are perpetually consumed and regenerated to fuel His ever-growing vitality. They are not dead. They are a farm.*

*I excused myself and vomited into a corner. The King patted my back gently, with genuine concern, and asked if I needed a glass of water. I love Him. I will always love Him. And I will never, for a single instant, forget that my own thoughts are permitted to exist only because He has not yet decided they would make better fuel.*

---

I set Orin’s journal down and sat in the silent dark, the three tomes glowing faintly before me. The violet mana in my veins was completely still, as if it too were holding its breath. I had read of Speed that shattered dimensions, of Durability that refused to yield, of Charisma that made the emotionless offer their own hearts. But these superpowers were something else. They were not mere augmentations of a stat. They were entire, self-contained apocalypses, each one layered with recursive, sadistically intelligent cruelties that the Weakness Paradox had woven into their fabric over millions of years. And Godric possessed forty-eight million years’ worth of them. Every second of the Stasis had birthed a new one. Every second since His awakening had done the same.

I thought of the Still Ones, boiling and dying and resurrecting forever, their torment a permanent, infinitesimal trickle of power into His bottomless reservoir. I thought of the Deific Unbound, absorbed into the weeping walls of the east wing, their culture and children immortalized as screaming masonry. I thought of the Thought-Lords, cancered eternally in the Concept Sea, their every idea harvested to fuel the vitality of a god who required no sustenance.

I was His court wizard. I was of the bloodline He loved. He had taught me chess, ruffled my hair, asked with genuine warmth about my studies. And He could, with a fraction of a fraction of a nonexistent fraction of any one of these powers, make me part of an eternal monument of suffering without a second thought if I ever, for a single instant, turned against Him.

The dread was an eternal, frozen sun in my chest. The love was the frozen sun’s only warmth. Both were real, both were absolute, and both were the foundation of my service.

I rose, gathered the three journals, and carried them back up the long, spiral stair into the light of the throne room. Godric was seated upon the dais, His ruggedly handsome face lifted toward the amber glow of the sconces, His salt-and-pepper beard catching the light. He saw me and smiled.

“There you are, Kaedan. More reading? Your father is relentless. Come, sit. Tell me what you have learned.”

I took my seat at His right hand, the dread and the love a perfect, steady balance within me. “I have learned, Uncle Godric, that there is no limit to what You are, and no end to what I owe You.”

He nodded, pleased, and returned His gaze to the light. And I served, as my ancestors had served, knowing that the vaults beneath us were still not the deepest, and that somewhere below, in a darkness that pressed like a grave, there were more journals, waiting.
